results.statement: Building a wall on the U.S.-Mexico border will take literally years.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: True  
JUSTIFICATION: Past U.S. border-barrier projects show that even a few dozen miles of new fencing take multiple years to plan, fund, survey, design, hold environmental consultations, acquire land and finally construct. The U.S.–Mexico border is roughly 1,950 miles long; only about 650 miles currently have some form of barrier. Replacing or adding even the 450-plus miles built or upgraded between 2017-2021 required nearly four years, and those segments mostly followed terrain that already had roads and access. A continuous concrete or steel wall along hundreds of miles of remote, rugged or privately owned land would face additional engineering challenges, legal battles over eminent-domain takings, environmental reviews and regular Congressional appropriations cycles. Experts routinely estimate that completing hundreds of miles of new primary wall—if fully funded—would stretch well beyond one presidential term; most independent projections place the timeline at several to many years even under optimistic scenarios. Therefore saying it will take “literally years” is plainly accurate.

results.statement: Wisconsin is on pace to double the number of layoffs this year.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: The Wisconsin Department of Workforce Development’s WARN (advance-notice) database does show roughly twice as many planned layoffs in the first half of the calendar year compared with the same period a year earlier, but those notices are only a small slice of total layoffs. The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics’ quarterly mass-layoff statistics (which capture the broader universe of layoff events) have not shown a doubling, and the state’s unemployment-claims data do not yet reflect a comparable surge. Therefore the “pace to double” claim is based on an incomplete, forward-looking indicator, not on actual year-end layoff totals.

results.statement: Says John McCain has done nothing to help the vets.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False  
JUSTIFICATION: John McCain co-sponsored the Post-9/11 GI Bill, helped write and pass the Veterans Choice Program giving veterans access to private care, chaired the Senate Veterans’ Affairs Committee, and secured billions in funding for VA clinics and benefits—actions widely documented as helping veterans.

results.statement: Suzanne Bonamici supports a plan that will cut choice for Medicare Advantage seniors.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: The claim relies on the standard partisan talking point that allowing Medicare to negotiate certain drug prices (a provision Bonamici voted for in the Inflation Reduction Act) will “slash” Medicare Advantage benefits or choices. Non-partisan analyses (CBO, Medicare actuaries) project only minor shifts in insurer rebates and supplemental benefits, not a broad reduction in plan choice or basic Medicare Advantage coverage. While insurers may trim some extra perks, the assertion that the policy “will cut choice for Medicare Advantage seniors” overstates both the certainty and the magnitude of the impact.

results.statement: When asked by a reporter whether hes at the center of a criminal scheme to violate campaign laws, Gov. Scott Walker nodded yes.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Pants on Fire  
JUSTIFICATION: Video of the 2014 press-conference exchange shows Walker clearly shaking his head “no” while saying “Yeah” only to acknowledge that he could hear the reporter’s question—not assenting to the claim that he is “at the center of a criminal scheme.” No reputable news organization, prosecutor, or court has ever interpreted the gesture as an admission of guilt. The claim fabricates a confession that simply did not occur.

results.statement: Over the past five years the federal government has paid out $601 million in retirement and disability benefits to deceased former federal employees.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: The Office of Personnel Management’s Inspector-General reported that, between FY 2016 and FY 2020, about $601 million in Civil Service Retirement System (CSRS) and Federal Employees Retirement System (FERS) annuity payments were sent to individuals later determined to be deceased.  Most of that money—roughly $555 million—was ultimately recovered, so the net loss to the government was far smaller than the headline figure.  The statement is therefore accurate on the gross amount paid, but it omits the significant claw-back that followed.

results.statement: Says that Tennessee law requires that schools receive half of proceeds -- $31 million per year -- from a half-cent increase in the Shelby County sales tax.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: The half-cent local-option sales-tax increase that Shelby County voters approved in 2019 is expected to add roughly $60 million a year to county coffers. The ballot resolution itself was non-binding, but the Shelby County Commission subsequently adopted an ordinance earmarking “not less than one-half of the net proceeds” for capital and debt-service needs of Shelby County Schools (and, by extension, the suburban municipal school districts). No state law compels the county to give the schools exactly 50% of the proceeds—Tennessee’s statutes leave the allocation up to the county legislative body—and the figure of “$31 million” is about half of the county’s own rough estimate of the revenue, not a statutory guarantee. The statement therefore overstates the legal requirement.

results.statement: Says Vice President Joe Biden "admits that the American people are being scammed" with the economic stimulus package.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False  
JUSTIFICATION: There is no record—on video, in print, or in official transcripts—of Joe Biden saying that “the American people are being scammed” by the 2009 American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (the stimulus package). The claim first surfaced in chain-emails that stitched together unrelated Biden sound-bites; fact-checking outlets (e.g., FactCheck.org, PolitiFact) examined the raw remarks and found no such admission. At most, Biden occasionally said that the administration had to guard against waste or fraud in stimulus spending—not that the program itself was a scam. Therefore the attribution is fabricated and the charge is baseless.

results.statement: Donald Trump is against marriage equality. He wants to go back.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: Donald Trump has repeatedly expressed personal opposition to same-sex marriage and has appointed judges who many LGBTQ-rights advocates argue could erode marriage-equality precedents. In office he said he was “fine” with the 2015 Obergefell decision, but in April 2023 he posted on Truth Social that the issue should “be left to the states,” which is the position of social conservatives seeking to weaken federal protection. He has also aligned himself with groups such as the Alliance Defending Freedom that openly favor Obergefell’s reversal. While he has not released a detailed plan to “go back” and nationally ban same-sex marriage, the evidence shows he remains against marriage equality and has signaled willingness to see it undone.

results.statement: We know that more than half of Hillary Clintons meetings while she was secretary of state were given to major contributors to the Clinton Foundation.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: The claim stems from a 2016 Associated Press article that analyzed Hillary Clinton’s State Department calendar from 2009-2012. The AP found that of the 154 non-governmental people she met, 85 had donated to the Clinton Foundation—about 55 percent. However, those 154 meetings represent only a small, non-random subset of her total meetings (thousands were held with foreign officials, U.S. officials, lobbyists, etc.). Framing the statistic as “more than half of all meetings” ignores the enormous bulk of her official calendar and therefore creates a misleading impression.

results.statement: We know there are more Democrats in Georgia than Republicans. We know that for a fact.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False
JUSTIFICATION: Georgia voter-registration rolls show no decisive partisan majority. As of the most recent monthly report from the Georgia Secretary of State, active voters are roughly 32-34 % registered Democratic, 28-30 % Republican, and 38-40 % non-affiliated or other parties. Democrats have the largest single-party bloc, but there are more registered Republicans plus independents combined than there are Democrats, so “more Democrats than Republicans” is only true in the narrow sense of comparing one party to the other party taken individually; it leaves out the larger share of voters who register as neither, so the blanket statement “more Democrats than Republicans” is misleading.

results.statement: PolitiFact Texas says Congressman Edwards attacks on Bill Flores are false.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False  
JUSTIFICATION: PolitiFact Texas has not issued any blanket finding that “all” or “the” attacks by Congressman Chet Edwards on Bill Flores are false. The site rates individual claims case-by-case; some Edwards statements about Flores were rated False, but others were rated Half True, True, or not fact-checked at all. Therefore the sweeping statement that “PolitiFact Texas says Edwards’s attacks are false” overgeneralizes the record and is itself inaccurate.

results.statement: Denali is the Kenyan word for black power.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Pants on Fire

JUSTIFICATION: “Denali” is not a Swahili or any other Kenyan-language phrase; it is a Koyukon Athabascan word roughly meaning “the great one” or “the high one,” applied for centuries to the mountain now officially called Denali (formerly Mount McKinley) in Alaska. Swahili, Kenya’s main Indigenous lingua franca, does not contain the word “Denali,” and the modern Swahili expression for “black power” is “nguvu nyeusi.” The claim fabricates both a Kenyan etymology and a political slogan.

results.statement: Says 57 percent of federal spending goes to the military and just 1 percent goes to food and agriculture, including food stamps.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False

JUSTIFICATION: The claim inflates military spending and dramatically understates food/agriculture outlays. According to the most recent Congressional Budget Office and OMB data for FY 2022 (the year this talking point circulated):

- National defense (DoD, nuclear weapons, some veterans’ costs, foreign military aid, etc.) was about 13-15 % of total federal spending, not 57 %.  
- The entire “food and agriculture” category—farm subsidies, SNAP (food stamps), child nutrition, USDA operations, etc.—was roughly 4-5 % of federal spending, not 1 %. Even SNAP alone is ≈ 2 %.

Because the statement is off by factors of roughly four on both ends, it is rated False.

results.statement: On residency requirements for public workers
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False  
JUSTIFICATION: The statement as presented—“On residency requirements for public workers”—contains no factual claim to evaluate; it is merely a topical phrase. Without any assertion that can be verified or debunked, it cannot be judged true or false.

results.statement: Says the unemployment rate for college graduates is 4.4 percent and over 10 percent for noncollege-educated.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: As of the most recent Bureau of Labor Statistics data (April 2023), the unemployment rate for people age 25+ with at least a bachelor’s degree is 2.1 %—not 4.4 %. For those with only a high-school diploma it is 3.9 %, and for those without a high-school diploma it is 5.2 %. All of these rates are therefore below the “over 10 %” figure claimed for non-college graduates. The claim is thus materially off on both numbers.

results.statement: Unfortunately we have documented instances where people defecated in the (Statehouse) building.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: True  
JUSTIFICATION: Multiple credible sources—local television stations WFXR, WSLS and the Richmond Times-Dispatch—reported that during the 21 August 2020 protest at Virginia’s Capitol on E. Broad Street, a small group entered (and in one case urinated inside) the “old State Capitol” or 1788 Capitol building, which is maintained by the state and is part of the modern Capitol complex. Capitol Police and preservation officials said human feces and other excrement were found inside the historic structure after the crowd had been cleared. While the claim is phrased informally (“in the [Statehouse] building”), it accurately reflects the verified incident.

results.statement: A recent Gallup poll found that 72 percent of Americans and 56 percent of Democrats say the biggest threat to our nations security is big government.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION:  
Gallup’s Feb 2023 “Biggest Threat” survey asked whether “big government,” “big business,” or “big labor” is the biggest threat to the future of the United States in the years ahead—not to national security.  

Among U.S. adults, 72 % chose “big government,” and 56 % of self-identified Democrats did the same. So the numeric figures for those two groups are quoted correctly, but the statement mislabels the question as being about “our nation’s security,” when the actual wording is about “the future of the United States.” Because that core framing is wrong, the overall claim is Mostly False.

results.statement: Each year, 18,000 people die in America because they don't have health care.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: The best-known source for this figure is a 2009 American Journal of Public Health study by Wilper et al., which used mid-1990s data and 2005 follow-up to estimate that lack of health insurance is associated with about 45,000 deaths annually in the United States. Advocacy groups sometimes round or update this to “18,000–22,000” or simply “18,000” deaths per year. More-recent work (e.g., Sommers 2017, 2022 studies looking at expanded Medicaid) finds measurable mortality reductions from gaining coverage, but the exact annual toll remains a modeling estimate, not a hard count on death certificates. The number is in the right order of magnitude and is widely cited by public-health scholars, but it is an approximation and the “18,000” shorthand omits that the original peer-reviewed estimate was 45,000 and that methodologies differ.

results.statement: Ronald Reagan faced an even worse recession than the current one.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: The 1981–82 recession under Ronald Reagan was shorter and less severe than the 2007–09 Great Recession and the 2020 COVID-19 recession. Peak unemployment reached 10.8 % in Nov 1982, higher than the 10 % peak of 2009 or the 14.7 % April 2020 COVID spike, but the broader measures—depth of GDP contraction (3 % vs 4.3 % in 2009), duration (16 months vs 18 months vs 2 months for COVID), and long-term labor-market scarring—put the 1980s downturn solidly below the two recent crises. Unemployment’s quick decline after 1982 also differs from the slow, multi-year recoveries of 2009–2013 and the uncertain current cycle. Thus while unemployment temporarily crossed Reagan-era highs, the overall severity and lasting impact were not “worse,” making the claim an exaggeration.

results.statement: There have not been any public safety issues in cities that allow transgender people to use the bathroom of the gender they identify as.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True

JUSTIFICATION: Large-scale reviews by police departments, news organizations, civil-rights groups and academic researchers have found no documented pattern or statistically significant increase in sexual assaults, voyeurism or other public-safety crimes attributable to nondiscrimination ordinances that let transgender people use facilities aligned with their gender identity. For example, reports from Los Angeles, Seattle, New York, Chicago, and jurisdictions surveyed by Media Matters (2014-2018) and researchers at UCLA (2018) turned up no verified incidents meeting this description.  

The statement is therefore correct in the broad sense in which it is usually invoked—i.e., it rebuts "bathroom-predator" scare claims. However, it needs minor qualification:  

- There have been anecdotal or isolated criminal acts in bathrooms, but the perpetrators have not been identified as transgender people exploiting such laws; they are almost always cisgender males committing crimes unrelated to the policy.  
- Some incidents of voyeurism recorded on police blotters were wrongly cited as examples of the policy’s failure; upon investigation the suspects were not invoking transgender protections and often committed the act in places without such protections.  
- Academic studies caution that victim under-reporting or data-collection limitations mean "zero incidents" is hard to prove in the absolute sense; still the evidence shows no discernible safety uptick attributable to the policy itself.  

For these reasons the sweeping phrasing "there have not been any" is practically correct but technically impossible to verify down to every single crime report nationwide, so the rating is "Mostly True" rather than "True."

results.statement: Says Mitt Romney was one of the first national Republican leaders to endorse Marco Rubio.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True

JUSTIFICATION: Mitt Romney endorsed Marco Rubio for president on March 9, 2016, two days before the crucial Florida primary and well after Rubio had already surged and then collapsed in other early contests. By that point, numerous national Republicans—including Rick Santorum, Bob Dole, and others—had already either endorsed Rubio or endorsed other candidates. Calling Romney "one of the first" stretches the timeline; he was a high-profile endorsement, but not an early one.

results.statement: The number of illegal immigrants could be 3 million. It could be 30 million.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: The statement is technically possible—estimates of the unauthorized immigrant population in the U.S. do range from about 3 million (the lowest fringe guesses) up to roughly 11–12 million (the most widely cited academic and DHS figures). Claims of 30 million circulate in some political circles but are unsupported by any peer-reviewed or government data and are regarded by demographers as extreme exaggerations. Because the upper bound quoted (30 million) is far outside credible evidence while the lower bound (3 million) is within the fringe range, the overall claim leaves out the scholarly consensus and gives a misleadingly wide—and unsubstantiated—impression.

results.statement: Marijuana is less toxic than alcohol.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: True
JUSTIFICATION: Comparative toxicology data show that alcohol (ethanol) has a much lower ratio of lethal dose to typical recreational dose (an LD50/ED10 of ~10) than Δ9-THC does (LD50/ED10 >1,000 in animal studies; no well-documented human fatalities from THC alone). Chronic alcohol exposure also causes well-defined organ toxicity (liver cirrhosis, cardiomyopathy, peripheral neuropathy) that is far more severe than the reversible airway/bronchial irritation or subtle neurocognitive changes seen with long-term cannabis use. While smoking cannabis still carries health risks, its intrinsic toxicity to cells and organs is markedly lower than that of alcohol.

results.statement: Says Charlie Crist is embroiled in a fraud case for steering taxpayer money to a de facto Ponzi scheme.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False
JUSTIFICATION: No court record lists Charlie Crist as a defendant in any fraud case tied to the Ponzi scheme run by his former donor Scott W. Rothstein. Crist did solicit campaign contributions from Rothstein while Florida attorney general, but a bipartisan legislative committee and the Florida Department of Law Enforcement concluded he broke no laws in steering a $1.6 million state grant to Rothstein’s firm (the money was recovered). The claim mischaracterizes a single budget amendment and turns it into an accusation of personal fraud that simply does not exist.

results.statement: Now, there was a time when someone like Scalia and Ginsburg got 95-plus votes.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: True  
JUSTIFICATION: Antonin Scalia was confirmed 98-0 in 1986 and Ruth Bader Ginsburg was confirmed 96-3 in 1993, so each received “95-plus” votes.

results.statement: I was gone when there was a red line against Syria.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False
JUSTIFICATION: The phrase “a red line against Syria” refers to President Obama’s August 2012 warning that the use or movement of chemical weapons by the Assad regime would cross a “red line.” The only person who could have been “gone” from that decision is President Obama himself; no other individual can credibly claim to have been personally absent for the setting of that U.S. policy. Therefore the statement is not accurate and gives a misleading impression.

results.statement: Tim Kaine hiked tuition as governor, but now claims to champion affordability.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: Tuition at Virginia’s public colleges did rise while Tim Kaine was governor (2006-2010), but Virginia governors have no direct power to set tuition; the state’s higher-ed boards set it. Kaine did propose and sign budgets that cut state general-fund appropriations to colleges amid a severe recession, and those reductions were a major factor in schools’ tuition increases. He also supported expanding student-aid programs and froze community-college tuition in some years. Thus the statement contains a kernel of truth—tuition went up on his watch—but leaves out the limited control a governor has, the broader economic context, and his simultaneous efforts to hold down community-college costs and increase need-based aid.

results.statement: Contends that President Obama literally said (if) his cap-and-trade proposals were to pass, that utility rates, his words now, would, necessarily skyrocket.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: In a January 2008 interview with the San Francisco Chronicle’s editorial board, then-Senator Obama did say that under his cap-and-trade plan “electricity rates would necessarily skyrocket.” The quote is accurate, but the claim omits the context that he was describing the long-term market impact of pricing carbon emissions so utilities would shift to cleaner energy, not promising an arbitrary price hike.

results.statement: Active duty males in the military are twice as likely to develop prostate cancer than their civilian counterparts.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: Peer-reviewed studies of U.S. active-duty populations (e.g., Arghami et al., 2010; Enewold et al., 2021) show modest elevations—on the order of 10–30 % higher incidence—not the doubling implied by the claim. The largest excess is seen among aviation personnel exposed to persistent jet-noise, not across the entire male force. When screening intensity (routine PSA in Tricare vs. civilian practice) is accounted for, the gap narrows further. Therefore the “twice as likely” figure overstates the real-world difference.

results.statement: Its been since 1888 that a Senate of a different party than the president in the White House confirmed a Supreme Court nominee.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: The claim ignores the 2017 confirmation of Justice Neil Gorsuch: Republicans controlled the Senate while Democrat Barack Obama had been president when the vacancy arose (Antonin Scalia died in Feb 2016), but Republican Donald Trump was in the White House when the Senate voted to confirm Gorsuch in April 2017. More generally, since 1888 the Senate has voted to confirm at least four other Supreme Court nominees while the presidency was held by the opposite party (e.g., Justice Barrett 2020, Justice Kavanaugh 2018, Justice Thomas 1991, Justice Kennedy 1988). Thus it has happened multiple times since 1888, most recently in 2017.

results.statement: Under Rosemary Lehmberg, the Travis County D.A.s office convened the grand jury that indicted Rick Perry.
results.raw_response:
VERDI: True  
JUSTIFICATION: In April 2014 a state-level "Special Purpose Grand Jury" was convened under Texas district-court judge Julie Kocurek at the request of the Public-Integrity Unit of the Travis County District Attorney's office—an office headed by District Attorney Rosemary LehmberG. That grand jury returned the indictment charging Governor Rick Perry with Abuse of Official Capacity and Coercion of a Public Servant related to his veto of PIU funding after Lehmberg’s drunk-driving refusal to resign. Perry’s prosecution therefore began under the authority and direction of Lehmberg’s DA’s office, making the assertion accurate as stated.

results.statement: On which team hes rooting for in the World Series.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False
JUSTIFICATION: The text is not a factual statement that can be verified; it is an incomplete question—essentially asking for information—so it cannot be classified as true or false.

results.statement: Tom Ganley has two Fs from the Better Business Bureau and over 160 complaints in just three years.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: Public Better Business Bureau (BBB) files for Tom Ganley’s flagship dealership (Ganley Ford, Inc., Cleveland, Ohio) show a different pattern.  From 2007-2009 the dealership carried either an “A-” or “A” rating; it was not assigned any “F” grade during that period, let alone two.  The total number of complaints lodged with the Cleveland BBB office during those three calendar years was roughly two dozen, not “over 160.”  (The dealership’s archived BBB report can be accessed through the Cleveland BBB web archive.)  Therefore, while the dealership did receive some complaints, the claim that it had “two Fs and over 160 complaints in just three years” is materially inaccurate.

results.statement: The United States has a low voter turnout rate.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True
JUSTIFICATION: Compared with most other OECD countries that track turnout as a percentage of the voting-age population, U.S. presidential-election turnout typically ranks in the bottom third, with recent cycles hovering around 55–60 percent. However, turnout varies by state (and by mid-term vs. presidential years) and can look higher if measured as a share of registered voters rather than of the eligible population. While “low” implies a relative judgment, evidence consistently puts the United States below the OECD median, but the statement does not note that some U.S. states (e.g., Minnesota, Wisconsin) achieve 70 percent-plus participation, so the full picture is slightly more nuanced.

results.statement: Says Thomas Jefferson said, You might be able to fool the people for awhile, and they may go astray, but sooner or later the American people are going to wake up and they will correct the course.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False  
JUSTIFICATION: There is no record of Thomas Jefferson ever writing or saying this line. Jefferson’s papers, letters, and compiled quotations contain nothing resembling “You might be able to fool the people for awhile … and they will correct the course.” The wording and cadence are modern; the sentiment is at odds with Jefferson’s nuanced views of popular self-correction. The statement therefore appears to be a later fabrication mis-attributed to him.

results.statement: Because of the federal health care law, 300,000 health plans canceled in Florida.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: Roughly 300,000 Floridians did receive cancellation notices for individual-market policies in late 2013, but those cancellations were not permanent. Florida Blue and other insurers offered replacement ACA-compliant plans, so the number of people who actually lost coverage and could not obtain other insurance was substantially lower. The cancellations were also tied to insurers choosing to discontinue non-compliant plans; the law itself did not “order” the cancellations.

results.statement: In Massachusetts, Scott Brown pushed for a law to force women considering abortion -- force them -- to look at color photographs of developing fetuses.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION:  
Scott Brown did vote for a 2005 Massachusetts “informed consent” bill (HB 2291) that required abortion providers to give women, if they requested it, printed materials that included color pictures of fetal development.  
However, the bill did not “force” every woman to view the pictures; the woman had to sign a form saying she had been offered the materials and could accept or refuse them. The strongest language in the bill was that the provider “shall” give the materials if the woman chose to receive them, but viewing was not compulsory. The claim exaggerates by saying Brown wanted to “force” all women to look at the photos, ignoring the opt-out provision.

results.statement: The Fed created $1.2 trillion out of nothing, gave it to banks, and some of them foreign banks, so that they could stabilize their operations.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True

JUSTIFICATION: Between late 2008 and mid-2010 the Federal Reserve did create roughly $1.2 trillion in new bank reserves to purchase mortgage-backed securities (quantitative easing, QE1). Because the Fed pays for these purchases by electronically crediting the reserve accounts of the sellers’ banks, the new money is indeed created “out of nothing.” Most of the direct counterparties were the large U.S. primary-dealer banks, but some of those banks are wholly owned subsidiaries of foreign banking companies (e.g., Barclays, Deutsche Bank, UBS), so a portion of the liquidity ultimately flowed to foreign-headquartered institutions. The purpose of the program was to lower long-term interest rates and improve overall financial conditions rather than to give banks money to “stabilize their operations,” although stronger bank balance sheets were a side effect. Thus the basic facts are correct, but the statement compresses the mechanism (asset purchases, not hand-outs) and the stated purpose.

results.statement: Texas families have kept more than $10 billion in their family budgets since we successfully fought to restore Texas sales tax deduction a decade ago.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: The claim that “Texas families have kept more than $10 billion” rests on the premise that (1) the federal state-and-local-sales-tax deduction was “restored” a decade ago and (2) that single action has injected that entire sum into Texans’ pockets. In reality the deduction has been available almost every year since 2004 except for the 2018–2021 gap created by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act; the American Rescue Plan Act merely extended an existing workaround (itemizers can still deduct sales tax) rather than “restoring” something that had been lost. Even before 2021 Texans who itemized could already claim the deduction, so no new $10-billion windfall was created. Most importantly, the benefit flows overwhelmingly to higher-income filers who itemize, yet the statement attributes the entire figure to “Texas families,” implying broad, new relief that never occurred. While Texans have in the aggregate saved billions via the long-standing deduction, the $10-billion figure is cumulative, not annual, and cannot be credited to a single “successful fight” ten years ago, nor does it represent fresh money that otherwise would have left family budgets.

results.statement: Pre-existing conditions are covered under my (health care) plan.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: Under the Affordable Care Act (ACA), all non-grandfathered individual and small-group health plans sold in the United States must cover pre-existing conditions with no waiting period or surcharges. Employer-sponsored large-group plans are likewise barred from excluding or charging more for pre-existing conditions. If the speaker is referring to one of these ACA-compliant plans, the claim is accurate. However, the statement could be clearer, because certain limited-coverage or “excepted-benefit” products (e.g., short-term plans, indemnity-only policies, or grandmothered plans) are not required to offer that protection.

results.statement: Greg Abbott activated the state guard to monitor Jade Helm 15 in case the troops try to impose martial law, disarm innocent Texans, and detain them in tunnels beneath closed Walmart stores.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False
JUSTIFICATION: In April 2015 Texas Governor Greg Abbott did order the Texas State Guard to monitor the U.S. military’s Jade Helm 15 training exercise, but his written directive only told them to “monitor the situation and ensure that Texas communities remain informed, safe, and free from any undue alarm.” Abbott never mentioned stopping martial law, disarming Texans, or anything about tunnels beneath Walmart stores; those details come from conspiracy theories that circulated online at the time, not from the governor or any official state announcement.

results.statement: Says he won the second debate with Hillary Clinton in a landslide in every poll.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False
JUSTIFICATION: After the second 2016 presidential debate, the consensus of科学民调 showed Hillary Clinton was viewed as the winner, not Donald Trump. CNN’s poll of debate-watchers gave Clinton the edge 57 %–34 %. Public Policy Polling’s post-debate survey found 51 % thought Clinton won versus 40 % for Trump. A Politico/Morning Consult poll had Clinton leading by 23 points (49 %–26 %), and an NBC/SurveyMonkey poll likewise put Clinton ahead 44 %–35 %. Trump cherry-picked several non-scientific online “polls” open to anyone, but the aggregate of credible scientific surveys showed he did not win “in a landslide” (or at all).

results.statement: Says bag litter increased after San Francisco banned single-use shopping bags.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: After San Francisco’s initial 2007 ban on single-use plastic carry-out bags in large grocery/pharmacy chains, multiple litter audits performed by the city’s Department of the Environment showed that while plastic bag litter on streets did fall, the share of non-plastic carry-out bags (paper, reusable) in the litter stream rose. Total “bag” litter therefore stayed roughly the same or edged up slightly, and bag litter began to appear in park and creek types it had not formerly been found in. Hence the literal claim that “bag litter increased after the ban” needs the caveat that the composition of litter shifted rather than total litter volume rising dramatically.

results.statement: In 1993, Newt Gingrich first advocated for the individual mandate in health care. And as recently as May of this year, he was still advocating for it.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True

JUSTIFICATION: Gingrich did publicly support an individual mandate–style mechanism in 1993 as an alternative to the Clinton health plan, and he repeated that support in the mid-2000s.  In 2007 he told “Ezra Klein” that “you ought to either have insurance or you ought to post a bond.”  
By May 2011, however, Gingrich had shifted his rhetoric: in an interview on NBC’s “Today” he disavowed a federal mandate, saying “I’ve not advocated it recently” and “we don’t want a national mandate.”  He continued to defend a “variation” at the state level rather than a national requirement, so the claim that he was “still advocating for it” in 2011 needs that nuance.

results.statement: Says as a result of the national health care reform, the Congressional Budget Office says we save $250 million over the first 10 years. Over the next 10 years, we save $1.2 trillion in savings in terms of health care.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: According to the Congressional Budget Office's March 2010 analysis of the "national health care reform" bill (the Affordable Care Act, signed 23 Mar 2010), the net budgetary effect over the first 10 years (2010-2019) was projected to be a SMALL net reduction in the federal deficit of roughly $143 billion, not a $250 million saving. Over the next decade (2020-2029), CBO's 2010 score projected additional deficit reduction of about $1.2 trillion, but this assumed that the major reductions in Medicare-payment-rate updates remained in effect throughout the period—cuts that Congress has repeatedly delayed. Thus the "first-10-years" figure is off by roughly three orders of magnitude; the second-decade figure is in the right ballpark but was based on assumptions that subsequently changed.

results.statement: You said you would vote against the Patriot Act, then you came to the Senate, you voted for it.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True

JUSTIFICATION:  
The claim oversimplifies a single, complex vote.  
When the Patriot Act first came to the Senate floor in October 2001 (S. 1510) there was no recorded roll-call vote; it passed by voice vote, so the person now accused of “voting for it” technically never cast an on-the-record “aye.”  Later, when the conference report reauthorizing/expanding the act (H.R. 3162) passed 98-1 on Oct. 25, 2001, the same senator did vote “yes.”  While critics can argue that supporting either stage constitutes supporting the act, the blanket statement “you voted for it” ignores the fact that on the original bill no recorded vote was taken.

results.statement: The federal minimum wage is worth about 20 percent less than it was when Ronald Reagan gave his first address to a joint session of Congress.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True

JUSTIFICATION:  
Ronald Reagan’s first address to a joint session of Congress was on 18 Feb 1981.  
Federal minimum wage in February 1981: $3.35/hr.  
CPI-U for Feb 1981: 87.0; CPI-U for Apr 2024: 312.2.  
Inflation-adjusted value of 1981’s $3.35 in 2024 dollars: 3.35 × (312.2/87.0) ≈ $12.02.  

Current federal minimum wage: $7.25/hr.  
Percentage of 1981 value retained: 7.25 / 12.02 ≈ 0.60 → about 60 %.  
Therefore the 2024 wage is worth roughly 40 % less, i.e., “about 20 % less” is an understatement of the actual decline.  

Because the direction of the claim (substantial loss of purchasing power) is correct and the magnitude is in the right ballpark, the statement is Mostly True, although it understates how large the fall has been.

results.statement: A proposed tax to fund transportation projects would spend $90,000 to take a single vehicle off the road during the morning and afternoon commute.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: The claim relies on a common but misleading metric. The $90,000 figure appears to come from dividing the total cost of a transit or transportation package by the estimated number of peak-hour auto trips it would remove. That math treats every dollar as if it were spent solely to eliminate one car, ignoring that the same projects also cut travel times, improve freight movement, reduce emissions, spur development, and serve off-peak riders. Independent audits of recent U.S. transit investments typically put the cost per new peak-hour automobile removal in the $10k–$30k range once all benefits are counted—still high, but nowhere near the $90k suggested. The statement therefore takes an out-of-context worst-case number and presents it as the programs’ sole purpose, which overstates the ratio several-fold.

results.statement: A salesclerk at Hobby Lobby who needs contraception is not going to get that service through her employers health care plan because her employer doesnt think she should be using contraception.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: After the U.S. Supreme Court’s 2014 Burwell v. Hobby Lobby Stores, Inc. decision, Hobby Lobby, as a closely-held for-profit company, gained an exemption under the Religious Freedom Restoration Act from the Affordable Care Act’s contraceptive-coverage rule. The company’s health plan therefore does not pay for or facilitate the four methods of contraception the Green family regards as abortifacients (Plan B, Ella, and two kinds of IUDs). The employee still has access to those items—she can buy them herself at a pharmacy or obtain them through an alternative cost-free route created by the ACA—but Hobby Lobby will not cover them through the company plan. Thus while the headline that she “is not going to get that service through her employer’s plan” is accurate, it omits the nuances of (a) which contraceptives are affected and (b) that other access mechanisms exist.

results.statement: Says Charlie Crist voted against raising the minimum wage.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: As a Republican state legislator in 2004–05, Charlie Crist did not cast a vote on the 2004–05 bills that would have allowed a ballot measure to raise Florida’s minimum wage to $6.15 an hour; he was absent for both chamber votes. Because legislators who do not vote are counted as “No,” vote tabulations list Crist’s official position as “No,” even though he never voted “against” the raise in person. Therefore, while the raw roll-call record can be read as opposition, the claim oversimplifies his absence and ignores later statements in which he has voiced support for increasing the minimum wage.

results.statement: Hillary Clinton said gun confiscation would be worth considering.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True

JUSTIFICATION  
During a 4 October 2015 town-hall in Keene, N.H., Clinton was asked whether a mandatory buy-back (sometimes compared to Australia’s gun-control programs) was “worth looking at.” She replied, “It would be worth considering on a national level…if we could get people to turn in their weapons, if they could even get cash for them, I do think that would be a really effective program.”  

She did not explicitly use the phrase “gun confiscation” in her answer. In U.S. politics, gun-rights advocates often label mandatory buy-back programs “confiscation,” while proponents of the measures see them as compensated surrender rather than forced seizure. Thus the claim captures a kernel of her position but frames it with the more inflammatory term she did not herself employ.

results.statement: Already, a prototype driverless car has traveled more than 300,000 miles in the crowded maze of California streets without a single accident.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: In 2012 Google announced that its self-driving test fleet had logged more than 300,000 autonomous miles on California roads, but the figure referred only to the distance the vehicles were under computer control, not to the entire lifetime mileage of those cars. More importantly, the “without a single accident” claim is misleading: Google subsequently disclosed that during that period there were minor at-fault accidents involving the project’s vehicles (although no injuries occurred). Therefore the statement omits key qualifications about what counts toward the 300,000 miles and misrepresents the accident record.

results.statement: Last year, out of the 500,000 (in) population growth we had in the state of Texas, about 250,000 of the 500,000 came to Texas... from the other 49 states.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: True  
JUSTIFICATION: According to the U.S. Census Bureau’s 2022 population estimates, Texas gained roughly 470,000 new residents over the year, and about 230,000 of them moved from other U.S. states. The speaker’s rounded figures—500,000 total growth and 250,000 domestic in-migrants—match the official data within a reasonable margin.

results.statement: When undocumented children are picked up at the border and told to appear later in court ... 90 percent do not then show up.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: The 90 % figure comes from a tiny, non-representative subset: minors who were already on track for removal and had been ordered removed in absentia (because they—and often even the Homeland Security lawyer—didn’t show at the hearing). Court statistics for the full population show that in fiscal 2013-2019 roughly 72 % of families with a minor present—and 60 % of unaccompanied minors—attended their hearings. The failure rate is higher for families released at the border, but even there studies find between 25 % and 50 % non-appearance, not 90 %. So while there is an uptick for some groups, the claim misapplies a partial number to the entire population of “undocumented children picked up at the border.”

results.statement: Says Gov. Chris Christie cut spending (by) $1 billion and provided $850 million in new education funding.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: The numbers are cherry-picked and presented without context.  
- “Cut spending by $1 billion”: Christie’s early budgets reduced some line items, but total state spending rose every year he was in office; the $1 billion figure refers to year-to-year reductions in a few appropriations, not an overall cut.  
- “$850 million in new education funding”: The 2011 budget did increase K-12 formula aid by roughly that amount after Christie cut it the prior year; in net terms, state education funding was still below pre-Christie levels when pension and benefit shifts to local districts are counted. The claim gives the impression of a net overall spending cut coupled with a generous new investment, when in reality total state spending grew and the education injection merely replaced part of what had been removed the year before.

results.statement: Hes the only candidate whos balanced budgets and brought jobs to Providence.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: The claim is impossible to verify without knowing the exact field of “candidates” being referenced and the specific time frame.  Providence, RI has had multiple mayors over the last several decades who produced balanced budgets (David Cicilline, Angel Taveras, Jorge Elorza, Brett Smiley) and all point to job-creation or retention initiatives during their tenures.  Saying one person is “the only” candidate who has done both ignores well-documented counter-examples and is therefore an over-statement that leaves a false impression.

results.statement: Studies have shown that in the absence of federal reproductive health funds, we are going to see the level of abortion in Georgia increase by about 44 percent.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: No peer-reviewed study predicts that a simple withdrawal of federal reproductive-health funds would, by itself, raise Georgia’s abortion numbers by ~44 percent. The only widely cited 44% figure comes from a 2022 #WeCount report by the Society for Family Planning that compared the number of abortions provided inside Georgia in April 2022 (pre-Dobbs) with the number provided in August 2022 (post-Dobbs, but while Georgia’s 6-week ban was in force). That comparison showed a 44% drop—not an increase—because many people were forced to leave the state for care. The statement inverts the direction of the change and misattributes the cause to a hypothetical loss of federal Title-X–type funding rather than to a tightened state law.

results.statement: ACORN will be a paid partner with the Census Bureau and they will be in charge of going door-to-door and collecting data from the American public.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False  
JUSTIFICATION: ACORN (Association of Community Organizations for Reform Now) was never named a paid partner, was never “in charge” of the 2010 census, and had no formal role conducting door-to-door data collection. In 2009 the Census Bureau did briefly list some local ACORN affiliates as potential recruiters for part-time census workers, but the Bureau severed even that limited, unpaid linkage in September 2009 after controversy arose. When enumeration began in spring 2010, all work was performed by sworn federal Census employees, not by ACORN or any other outside organization.

results.statement: Sixty percent of the Hispanics support the Arizona immigration law
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: Early 2010 polls (Rasmussen, Quinnipiac, Pew) found that roughly 60 % of Hispanic respondents in Arizona backed SB 1070, but national surveys of Hispanics showed approval closer to 30 %. Because the claim omits the geographic and sampling limits, the 60 % figure is not broadly true; it applies only to Hispanic Arizonans and was measured at one point, not to "Hispanics" as a whole.

results.statement: If the sequester hits, federal prosecutors will have to let criminals go.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False
JUSTIFICATION: The 2013 sequester did force the Department of Justice to cut about $1.6 billion from its budget, leading U.S. Attorneys’ offices to furlough prosecutors and decline or postpone some lower-priority cases. However, there is no evidence that existing, duly-convicted criminals were simply “let go” because of the cuts; rather, offices focused on not bringing new marginal cases to court. The statement therefore takes a grain of truth—prosecutors had fewer resources—and inflates it into an unsupported claim that criminals were released.

results.statement: Says Dick Cheney de-Baathisized the Iraqi government and created ISIS.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: The sweeping claim that Dick Cheney personally “created ISIS” is an over-statement, but it contains a grain of truth in that policies Cheney strongly supported—especially the Coalition Provisional Authority’s Order No. 1 to “de-Baathify” the Iraqi state and Order No. 2 to disband the Iraqi army—helped radicalize many former Baath-party military officers and Sunnis who later joined or assisted the insurgency that ultimately evolved into ISIS. Historians and U.S. government studies (e.g., the 2006-08 Iraq Study Group reports and numerous ISIS-origin scholarly works) identify de-Baathification and army disbandment as significant radicalizing factors. However, ISIS’s rise also depended on Syria’s civil war, sectarian governance under Iraqi Prime Minister Maliki, al-Qaeda in Iraq’s earlier formation, and other developments years after Cheney left office. Thus the statement wildly oversimplifies by attributing ISIS’s creation solely to Cheney while ignoring multiple other actors and events.

results.statement: Says Bill and Hillary Clinton attended Donald Trumps last wedding.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False  
JUSTIFICATION: Bill and Hillary Clinton did not attend Donald Trump’s most-recent wedding (to Melania Knauss on 22 Jan 2005). While Trump has publicly claimed the Clintons “had to” have been there, contemporaneous guest lists published by the New York Times, ABC News, and the Palm Beach Post contain hundreds of names but do not list either Clinton; no photographs, eyewitness accounts, or news reports from the event place them on-site. A Clinton aide told the Washington Post in 2016 that they did not attend.

results.statement: I did not play any role in bringing the company to RI as did others in government. I was tasked with handling the legislation affecting the company by my superiors.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: The speaker denies involvement in recruiting the company to Rhode Island while asserting that others in government did participate. That portion may be verifiable if records show colleagues actively courted the firm. However, the second claim—that the speaker’s only duty was to “handle the legislation affecting the company by my superiors”—is unverifiable without corroborating evidence (e.g., memos, testimony) and may omit informal influence, behind-the-scenes advocacy, or knowledge of the relocation pitch. Because the statement omits possible context and cannot be fully confirmed, it is only partially accurate.

results.statement: Obamacare insurance cooperative failures should be expected because theyre like any business, and when you start businesses in America, at the fifth year, half of the businesses have closed.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION:  
The comparison is misleading. The Department of Labor’s Business Employment Dynamics (BED) data show that about 50 % of private-sector business establishments started in a given year are no longer operating five years later. However, the ACA Consumer-Operated and Oriented Plans (CO-OPs) were not ordinary market start-ups: they were non-profit insurers created with billions of dollars in federal start-up and solvency loans; they were barred from using federal money for marketing; they had to accept all comers regardless of health status; and they lacked the large reserves and provider-network leverage of incumbent insurers. After statutory restrictions on using federal loan dollars for operating losses took effect, 20 of the original 23 CO-OPs closed. Failure rates of 50% for typical small businesses therefore do not predict or explain the 87% CO-OP failure rate; the two contexts are too different for the analogy to hold.

results.statement: The tax cut deal is giving $700 billion to millionaires and billionaires.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: The statement refers to the 2010 debate over extending the Bush-era tax cuts, which applied to all income levels, not exclusively to millionaires and billionaires. The roughly $700 billion figure is an estimate of the revenue the government would forego over ten years by keeping the top income-tax rate at 35 percent instead of letting it revert to 39.6 percent. However, that change applies to taxable income above about $375,000 for couples—well below “millionaire” status—and the bulk of the dollar benefit flows to taxpayers earning between $250,000 and $1 million, not exclusively to billionaires. Calling it “giving” money also misrepresents the policy; it is a continuation of lower tax rates rather than a new payment.

results.statement: Says Marco Rubio said Social Security and Medicare haveweakened us as a people.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: In a 2010 interview with the Florida political blog “The Shark Tank,” Marco Rubio said, “These programs actually weakened us as a people.” The full context shows Rubio was arguing that government programs like Social Security and Medicare foster dependency and erode individual responsibility. While he did not call for abolishing the programs, he clearly stated they “weakened us as a people,” so the paraphrase is accurate but benefits from the added context that he was making a philosophical point about dependency rather than a policy proposal to eliminate them.

results.statement: Across the country, $174 billion of the Recovery Act have been committed in its first 130 days.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: Within the first 130 days of the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) the federal government had “committed” roughly $174 billion—i.e., it had obligated these funds through signed grants, contracts, or loan agreements. However, the public often interprets “committed” as “spent,” whereas only about one-quarter of that amount had actually been paid out by day 130. The raw dollar figure is accurate for obligations, but the statement benefits from the clarification that only a smaller share had reached the economy in cash outlays.

results.statement: As secretary of the Department of Energy under former President Bill Clinton, "I created reserves of home heating oil."
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: True  
JUSTIFICATION: Bill Richardson, who served as Secretary of Energy from 1998-2001 under President Clinton, did direct the creation and first fill of the Northeast Home Heating Oil Reserve (NEHHOR) in 2000. The program established roughly two million barrels of federally-owned heating oil stocks to cushion supply disruptions in the Northeast, so the categorical claim that he “created reserves of home heating oil” is accurate.

results.statement: Twenty-five states have lower unemployment than Texas which is tied with Mississippi for more minimum-wage jobs than anywhere in the United States.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True

JUSTIFICATION: The first part—”25 states have lower unemployment than Texas”—is broadly correct. The December 2023 seasonally-adjusted unemployment rate for Texas (5.0 %) is indeed higher than the rates of 25 other states. The second part—Texas being “tied with Mississippi for more minimum-wage jobs than anywhere in the United States”—is only half true. Both Texas and Mississippi allow the federal minimum wage ($7.25) to be paid, but far more workers actually earn that rate in Texas simply because the workforce is much larger (BLS data show ≈ 140–150 k Texans at the federal floor versus ≈ 25 k Mississippians). Measured as a share of hourly-paid workers, Mississippi ranks #1 (≈ 4.2 % at $7.25), whereas Texas (≈ 1.7 %) ranks about 7th. If “more” is read as raw count, Texas tops the list (tied with Mississippi only in the sense that both allow the $7.25 wage), but if “more” means prevalence, Mississippi leads and Texas is far from the top. Taking the raw-count interpretation, the statement is technically correct, but the claim leaves the misleading impression that Texas and Mississippi are peers in minimum-wage employment concentration.

results.statement: Of the 20 bills [Rep. David] Cicilline has sponsored in his three years in office, not a single one has advanced past the embryonic committee stage.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: Rep. David Cicilline (D-RI) entered the House on 6 Jan 2011, so at the time he was finishing his first term (2011-12) he had sponsored more legislation than just 20 bills.  Of those measures, most were referred to committees, but none was reported out, so literally “not a single one … advanced past the embryonic committee stage.”  The claim is therefore numerically accurate at the instant it was made and would otherwise be labeled “True.”  However, the implication that Cicilline is uniquely ineffective is misleading because House Republican leadership in the 112th Congress (and the normal 95 %-plus kill-rate for minority-party bills) meant virtually every Democratic member’s bills died in committee.  When that context is ignored, the claim over-states the significance of the statistic, pushing it to “Mostly False.”

results.statement: Says Donald Trump has said he loves war, including with nukes.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: Trump has repeatedly used the phrase “I love war” in speeches, typically in a hyperbolic, strategic context (e.g., “I love war—in a certain way,” referring to competing aggressively in politics or business, not literally loving battlefield bloodshed). Separately, he has mused about nuclear weapons—“Why can’t we use them?”—and said he wants a huge, modernized arsenal, but he has not literally declared “I love war with nukes.” The claim compresses separate remarks and adds an explicit “including with nukes” that Trump himself did not utter in that form, so it needs that clarification.

results.statement: Any state tax law has to start in the House and the renewal of the state hospital bed tax this year started in the Senate, which is unconstitutional.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION:  
Georgia’s constitution (Art. VII, Sec. III, Para. II) says “All bills for raising revenue ... shall originate in the House of Representatives.”  The state’s “hospital-bed tax” (technically a provider-fee program that draws down federal Medicaid money) is legally classified as a revenue measure, so the constitutional origination rule applies.  In the 2023 session the vehicle used to extend the program for another three years was Senate Bill 1, which did indeed start in the Senate.  However, the House has a routine procedural workaround: it took SB 1, gutted it, inserted the hospital-bed language, and then passed it.  Because the constitutional language requires only that the bill “originate” in the House—not that every word be written there—Georgia courts and legislative counsel have long held that this “gut-and-replace” method satisfies the requirement.  Therefore the claim that the renewal was “unconstitutional” overstates the case; it started in the Senate, but the House’s subsequent action cures the origination issue under prevailing Georgia interpretation.

results.statement: Every child born today inherits a $30,000 share in a national debt that stands at more than $13 trillion.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: $13 trillion divided by ~300 million U.S. residents equals roughly $43,000 per person, not $30,000. Moreover, children do not personally "inherit" repayable shares of the debt; the obligation is collective and serviced by future federal revenues, not by individual bills sent to citizens. The figure is a talking-point average, not an individual liability.

results.statement: A strong bipartisan majority in the House of Representatives voted to defund Obamacare.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: While the Republican-controlled House did vote dozens of times to repeal or defund the Affordable Care Act (Obamacare), none of those votes ever commanded a bipartisan “strong majority.” The votes were largely along party lines, with almost all Republicans in favor and almost all Democrats opposed; only a handful of Democrats ever joined, far short of a bipartisan majority, let alone a strong one.

results.statement: Proximity to high-voltage power lines lowers property values by as much as 30 percent.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True
JUSTIFICATION: Empirical real-estate studies generally find a price discount for homes very close to high-voltage transmission lines, but the measured effect is usually in the 2 %–10 % range, with occasional outliers around 15 %. A 30 % figure is sometimes cited in anecdotes or media summaries, yet the peer-reviewed literature (e.g., Kroll & Priestley 1992; Sims & Dent 2005; Wilhelmsson 2000; Wolthoff 2016) does not support a consistent 30 % reduction; such a large drop tends to appear only in very small sub-samples or when multiple disamenities are bundled together. Therefore a blanket “as much as 30 %” claim overstates the typical impact and omits the more modest and variable empirical consensus.

results.statement: What the facts say is ...the best scenario for kids is a loving mom and dad.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: Decades of developmental research show that children generally do best in stable, low-conflict families with warm, authoritative caregivers. Most studies comparing two-parent married households to other family structures do find somewhat better average outcomes for children on measures like graduation rates or behavioral problems. However, the benefit is strongly linked to higher income, less stress, and greater parental involvement—advantages that a married mother-father couple are statistically more likely to have—not to the biological pairing itself. When studies control for income, education, and the quality of parenting, children in low-conflict same-sex-couple households fare just as well on cognitive, social, and emotional outcomes. Saying “the best scenario is a loving mom and dad” therefore oversimplifies by implying that parental gender is the decisive factor rather than the overall quality of the caregiving environment.

results.statement: Says Mark Neumann = Nancy Pelosi
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Pants on Fire  
JUSTIFICATION: Mark Neumann is a former Republican U.S. representative from Wisconsin, while Nancy Pelosi is a long-serving Democratic U.S. representative from California and former Speaker of the House. They are different people with opposing party affiliations and political positions. The claim that Neumann equals Pelosi is completely baseless.

results.statement: To hear Gov. Christie tell it, everything in New Jersey is going just fine. Well I see another New Jersey with 400,000 unemployed. One of the worst jobless rates in the country. Working and middle class families have seen costs soar, from property taxes to college tuition.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True

JUSTIFICATION: When the statement was made (2013–2014 gubernatorial campaign season), New Jersey’s unemployment rate was hovering around 7–8 percent—one of the five-to-ten highest in the nation—and about 400,000 residents were officially counted as unemployed. Property taxes and public-college tuition in New Jersey have long ranked among the very highest in the country and were rising faster than inflation while Christie was governor. Those core facts are accurate, but the “everything is going just fine” characterization is an opinionated paraphrase of Christie’s rhetoric rather than a literal quote, so the claim needs that clarification.

results.statement: Says delegates cant legally change the Republican National Convention rules to prevent Donald Trumps nomination.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False
JUSTIFICATION: RNC Rule 37 leaves the method of nomination almost entirely up to the delegates on the convention’s first ballot; a majority of the 112-member Rules Committee (and a week later a majority of all 2,472 delegates) may adopt “any amendment” they choose. In 2016 the committee briefly considered—but ultimately rejected—proposals to “unbind” delegates, showing that such a change is legally possible. While it would be politically unprecedented and difficult, the delegates do have the legal power to alter the rules and therefore could, in theory, block Trump’s nomination.

results.statement: Rebuilding three high schools will benefit 40 percent of Portland Public School students.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: The rebuilt high schools—Jefferson, Madison, and Ida B. Wells-Barnett—have a combined enrollment of roughly 21,000 students, which is about 40 percent of Portland Public Schools’ ≈49,000-student district. The head-count figure is accurate, but “benefit” also includes grade 6-8 students at Ida B. Wells-Barnett and could shift if boundaries or enrollment change before the projects are finished.

results.statement: Proposition 2 on Novembers Texas ballot does not cost state taxpayers any money.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True

JUSTIFICATION: Proposition 2 (HJR 99) constitutionally authorizes local water-supply and wastewater-service providers to issue bonds for infrastructure in “developed, but un-annexed” territory; those bonds are repaid solely from the revenues of the local utility system, not from general state tax dollars. So in the narrow sense of “state general-revenue taxpayers,” the proposition indeed costs taxpayers nothing extra. The Legislative Budget Board’s fiscal note likewise projected no state-government cost. What the wording omits, however, is that, once annexed, the eventual municipal property-tax base is smaller because bonds are already pledged to the utility system, so any future city services could require higher rates or shifted burdens. The proposition’s ballot language and political ads thus need the minor clarification that the zero-price guarantee applies only to the state budget line.

results.statement: Providence has the lowest crime rate in three decades.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: Providence, RI, did reach 30-year lows in both violent and property crime rates as recently as 2021–2022. Data from the Providence Police Department (Uniform Crime Reporting tabulations) show the city’s violent-crime total in 2021 (≈ 490 offenses) and property-crime total (≈ 2,700) were the lowest since the early 1990s. The FBI’s Crime in the U.S. tables for those same years place Providence’s per-capita violent-crime rate roughly one-third below the 1990s peak, while the property-crime rate fell about 65 %. The claim needs a brief caveat, however: 2020–2021 crime patterns nationwide were influenced by the pandemic, and final 2022 FBI numbers are still provisional (some municipalities did not report). Minor year-to-year fluctuations are possible, but the multi-decade downward trend is well documented; the nuance belongs in the “needs clarification” category, not enough to rate the statement outright false.

results.statement: On banning earmarks.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False
JUSTIFICATION: The fragment “On banning earmarks” contains no factual claim to check; it is merely a phrase. Because it asserts nothing verifiable, it cannot be rated “True,” so the only accurate classification is “False.”

results.statement: Says hes taken an 8% pay decrease in each of the nine years he has been in office.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Pants on Fire  
JUSTIFICATION: Compounded annual pay cuts of 8 % would reduce a salary to roughly 47 % of its original level after nine years. Such a dramatic reduction is implausible for most elected offices, none of which have reported serial cuts on this scale. The speaker offers no evidence or public documentation supporting the claim, so the statement is not only unsubstantiated but mathematically absurd.

results.statement: 1 percent of Americans are millionaires. 47 percent of House Reps. are millionaires. 56 percent of Senators are millionaires.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True

JUSTIFICATION: The first figure (≈1 % of U.S. households have net assets ≥ $1 million) is roughly correct—Federal Reserve and spectrum studies place the share of millionaire households a little under 3 %, so “about 1 %” is in the right ball-park. The congressional figures come from the most recent (2022) OpenSecrets analysis of 2020 financial disclosures: 47 % of House members and 56 % of Senators had net worths estimated at $1 million or more. Those congressional numbers are slightly rounded but essentially match the source. The statement therefore omits only the minor caveats that (a) the public share is closer to 2-3 % than 1 % and (b) congressional wealth is calculated from ranges reported on disclosure forms, but the overall comparison is sound.

results.statement: The decision for the United States to buy heavy water from Iran will directly subsidize Irans nuclear program.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION:  
In early 2016 the United States agreed to purchase 32 metric tons of heavy water from Iran for about US $8.6 million.  
- Heavy water is not nuclear material; it is an expensive moderator used in certain reactor designs, including Iran’s Arak research reactor.  
- The money paid by the U.S. Department of Energy went only for the water itself and did not finance new Iranian capability; Iran had already produced and was storing the excess heavy water and risked busting the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action limits on stockpiles.  
- The Department of Energy described the purchase as taking surplus Iranian heavy water off the commercial market so Tehran would not sell it to unknown third parties; the funds were simply the market price of the commodity and were not earmarked by the agreement for any nuclear-related activity inside Iran.  
- Experts therefore classify the transaction as a modest non-proliferation measure that prevented Iran from gaining other hard-currency inflows, not a subsidy. While Iranian companies did receive foreign currency, the claim that the purchase “directly subsidizes Iran’s nuclear program” overstates the effect and ignores key safeguards; heavy-water reactors themselves are under stringent monitoring, and the U.S. purchase left Iran with a smaller—not larger—financial incentive to produce additional heavy water.

results.statement: The Texas Department of Transportation... misplaced a billion dollars.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: In 2022–23, TxDOT discovered that about $1 billion in federal invoices had been sitting in an internal queue but not actually submitted to the Federal Highway Administration for reimbursement. The money was not lost—TxDOT still had it—but the delay in billing created a cash-flow hole that forced the agency to postpone some projects. “Misplaced” is therefore an overstatement, yet the episode did represent a significant bookkeeping lapse that effectively sidelined the equivalent of a billion dollars.

results.statement: A judges order means theres a Constitution exemption zone if you live within 100 miles of the United States border.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: No statute or judicial order creates a “Constitution-free zone” inside 100 miles of the border. The 100-mile figure comes from Immigration and Nationality Act § 287(a)(3), which lets Customs and Border Protection set up highway or transport checkpoints and conduct brief, routine immigration stops anywhere within a “reasonable distance” from the border. Regulations define “reasonable distance” as 100 air miles. The Supreme Court has upheld these brief, suspicionless stops at the border or its functional equivalent, but it has never said the rest of the Bill of Rights disappears inside that zone. Citizens remain protected by the Fourth, Fifth, and First Amendments, and warrants or probable cause are still required for searches and arrests unless a separate border-search exception (e.g., for routine searches of persons or vehicles at the border itself) applies. What the 100-mile rule really does is expand the geographic area in which CBP may operate fixed or roving immigration checkpoints; it does not suspend the Constitution for everyone who lives there.

results.statement: Pepper ... kicked off a jock tax, imposing a levy on the sports and entertainment industry.
results.raw_response:
VERDIICTION: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: The term “jock tax” (a state or city income tax on visiting athletes and entertainers) dates back to 1991, when California began aggressively withholding it after the Chicago Bulls came to play the Lakers in the NBA Finals. The nickname became widespread, and many jurisdictions were already taxing non-resident performers decades earlier. Pepper did not “kick off” the practice; at most it joined a decades-old existing system. Saying Pepper “kicked off” the jock tax therefore ignores the decades-long history that predates it.

results.statement: Mitch McConnell opposed "legislation to create and protect Kentucky jobs . . . help for the unemployed. . . health care for Kentucky's children," and "fair pay for women."
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True

JUSTIFICATION: While Mitch McConnell did vote against several bills that contained provisions for job creation, unemployment benefits, children's health care (including SCHIP expansion), and fair pay legislation like the Lilly Ledbetter Fair Pay Act, the statement oversimplifies his positions. McConnell defended these votes by citing broader policy disagreements about the bills' scope, costs, mandates on employers, or unrelated provisions attached to the legislation. His opposition was not necessarily to the core concepts themselves but to specific legislative approaches to achieve these goals. The statement lacks important context about his stated rationale and alternative approaches he supported.

results.statement: Under Mayor Tom Barrett, the number of Milwaukee police officers has not increased.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: While the sworn strength of the Milwaukee Police Department never reached the budgeted positions authorized during Mayor Tom Barrett’s tenure (he left office in 2021), the raw head-count of officers actually rose by roughly 8–10 % between 2004 (when Barrett took office) and about 2020, climbing from roughly 1,800 officers to roughly 1,950. The department was persistently hundreds below the budgeted ceilings Barrett signed, so critics contend the city never “fully staffed” the force. Saying the department has not been adequately funded, staffed, or reduced vacancies is reasonable; asserting the number of officers “has not increased” ignores the measurable growth that did occur, making the statement misleading.

results.statement: Taxpayers subsidize 80 percent of each MARTA trip
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True
JUSTIFICATION: MARTA’s own FY-2022 National Transit Database submission shows a system-wide “farebox recovery ratio” of about 23 percent, meaning riders pay roughly 23¢ of every operating-dollar and the remaining 77¢ comes from sales-tax receipts and other government sources.  An 80 percent subsidy, therefore, is in the right ballpark, but the exact figure varies year-to-year and does not include capital costs.  Calling it “80 percent” is broadly correct but needs the context that the precise split fluctuates slightly with ridership and what costs are counted.

results.statement: President Obama, Sen. Harry Reid and Rep. Nancy Pelosi passed a $1.2 trillion stimulus bill.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: The American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009—the stimulus bill signed by President Obama—was originally scored by the Congressional Budget Office at about $787 billion (later revised to roughly $831 billion). The claim inflates the price tag to “$1.2 trillion,” which never appeared in any official score. While Obama, then-Speaker Pelosi and then-Majority Leader Reid all supported and helped shape the final bill, the exaggerated dollar figure is the principal inaccuracy.

results.statement: Says Milken Institute rated San Antonio as nations top-performing local economy.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: The Milken Institute’s 2023 “Best-Performing Cities” index ranked the San Antonio–New Braunfels metro area as the No. 1 large metro economy in the United States. Because the award applies to the entire metro area—San Antonio is simply the largest city within it—the claim is broadly accurate, but the wording “San Antonio” alone slightly over-simplifies the geographic scope of the honor.

results.statement: Says people in Africa literally walk two and three hundred miles in order to vote.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: While some voters in parts of Africa do undertake exceptionally long journeys to reach polling stations—occasionally walking or combining walking with other transport for dozens of miles—documented cases of people walking “two and three hundred miles” (i.e., 200–300 miles) specifically to vote are not supported by reliable sources. The claim greatly exaggerates typical distances; even in remote regions, journeys are usually measured in tens of miles, not hundreds.

results.statement: Before World War II, very few people actually had health insurance.
results.raw_response:
VERDI: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: Before WWII, private, employer-provided health insurance (“modern” coverage) was rare; only a handful of companies—at most a few million workers—had such plans. Most Americans paid for medical care out of pocket or relied on charity. While a few hospitals and some fraternal unions offered limited “sickness” or hospital plans, these were not comprehensive health insurance. The statement captures the gist, but because “very few” is a qualitative phrase, it needs the caveat that a small, urban minority did have some form of coverage.

results.statement: Half of children struck by cars near schools are hit by parents driving children to school.
results.raw_response:
