VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: From the early 1970s through the mid-2010s, Denmark’s suicide mortality (WHO and OECD data) has usually run 1½–2½ times the U.S. rate. For example, in 1970–1985 Denmark averaged ≈ 25–30 per 100 000 vs. 12–13 in the U.S.; even after both countries declined, Denmark still sat in the 8–11 range while the U.S. stayed around 10–12. Over the full five-decade window the Danish rate has indeed been close to “about twice” the American level most of the time, although the ratio has narrowed to roughly 1.3–1.4 by the late-2010s. Thus the claim is broadly accurate but slightly overstates the current gap.

results.statement: China has total control, just about, of North Korea.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: While China is North Korea’s largest trading partner, main aid source, and most important security backer—and it wields heavy influence over the regime’s survival—it does not have “total control.” Beijing has repeatedly refrained from fully curtailing Pyongyang’s weapons programs or preventing headline-grabbing provocations, and North Korea routinely defies Chinese requests, flouts UN sanctions Beijing voted for, and conducts missile launches that embarrass China. In 2017 alone, Kim Jong-un ignored several high-level Chinese warnings against nuclear and missile tests, demonstrating that Pyongyang operates on its own strategic calculus and is not a Chinese puppet. China’s leverage is large but constrained: severing aid could trigger regime collapse or war on its border, outcomes Beijing fears more than it values compliance. Therefore, the characterization of “total control” overstates Beijing’s actual authority and leaves out North Korea’s demonstrated independence.

results.statement: The fact is the Clinton Foundation has got about 80 percent in overhead and 20 percent of the money is actually getting into the places it should.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False  
JUSTIFICATION: Independent charity-rating organizations such as Charity Navigator and CharityWatch give the Clinton Foundation high marks for efficiency, not low ones. For 2016, CharityWatch assigned the foundation an “A” rating and calculated that roughly 88 % of its spending went to its programs rather than overhead; Charity Navigator likewise reported program-expense ratios above 80 %. The “80 % overhead / 20 % to programs” claim is therefore contradicted by the widely accepted financial data and falls far outside any reasonable margin of error.

results.statement: The U.S. Constitution owes its notion of democracy to the Iroquois Tribes.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: Historical evidence shows that some framers—not notably Benjamin Franklin—were familiar with the Iroquois Confederacy’s Great Law of Peace, a federal union that included principles of representative councils, checks against central power, and group decision-making. In 1988 the U.S. Congress passed a concurrent resolution acknowledging the Confederacy and “its contribution to the development of the United States Constitution.” However, the Constitution’s ideas were primarily rooted in European Enlightenment thought and colonial experience; no clause was copied from the Iroquois, and direct influence is debated among scholars. Thus the claim is broadly accurate but benefits from the clarification that Iroquois practices were one influence among many, not the original or defining source.

results.statement: Since the first of the year, 153 businesses at last count had moved out of California to Texas.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: The figure comes from a running tally kept by the business-relocation consultancy Spectrum Location Solutions. Their media-release “153 Tally of California Corporate Headquarters Departures and New Major Office Arrivals in Texas, January 2021 – November 2022” is the source most Texas officials cite. The same consultancy told the San Antonio Express-News that “a little over 150” headquarters moves had been documented through 2022, so the number 153 is not invented. However, the statement omits two caveats: (1) the count covers nearly two years, not “since the first of the year” if “the year” is the current one, and (2) it refers specifically to corporate headquarters, not every “business.” Because the numeric claim is in the right ball-park but the time frame and scope need context, it is Mostly True.

results.statement: Barack Obama has provided guns to Mexican drug cartels.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: The claim stems from the failed ATF “Operation Fast and Furious,” begun in 2009, in which U.S. agents knowingly allowed suspected traffickers to buy weapons so they could trace them to cartel leaders; hundreds of those guns were ultimately lost and some turned up at crime scenes in Mexico. However, the operation was run by Phoenix-based ATF and DOJ officials, not ordered or personally directed by President Obama. Multiple investigations (by the DOJ Inspector General, Congressional committees, and the ATF) found no evidence that Obama or Attorney General Holder authorized or had detailed prior knowledge of the specific tactics. Thus, while the federal government under Obama’s administration did allow guns to reach cartels, the statement that “Obama provided guns” implies direct presidential action that the record does not support.

results.statement: Says Chris Koster fell silentas attorney general in pursuing a case against a free classified service accused of promoting prostitution after accepting over $12,000 in campaign contributions from people affiliated with the service.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: The claim oversimplifies and misrepresents the situation. While Chris Koster did receive campaign contributions from individuals connected to Backpage (the classified service in question), there is no clear evidence that Koster "fell silent" or failed to pursue actions against the service as a result of those donations. In fact, several states, including Missouri under Koster’s leadership, took action against Backpage. Koster joined a bipartisan coalition of attorneys general in 2016 urging Congress to amend the Communications Decency Act to allow for prosecution of websites facilitating sex trafficking. The implication that Koster halted prosecution due to campaign donations lacks strong evidence and ignores broader legal and procedural complexities involved in prosecuting online platforms under federal law at the time.

results.statement: Health care premiums for consumers have doubled since 2001.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: The Kaiser Family Foundation’s annual Employer Health Benefits Survey shows that the average single-coverage premium that employers and workers paid rose from $2,889 in 2001 to $7,739 in 2022 (latest comparable year). While that represents an approximate 170% increase, the statement says “premiums for consumers.” Because the employer and the worker share cost, the share that actually comes out of a worker’s paycheck is far below a doubling (in 2001 the average worker contribution was ~$1,800; in 2022 it was ~$1,300). Even in the Affordable Care Act individual market, average unsubsidized benchmark premiums have roughly doubled since the market’s 2014 start, but the individual-market sample is smaller than the employer-based market and subsidies offset much of the increase for the majority of buyers. In short, total premium spending has more than doubled, but the “for consumers” wording omits the distinction between the sticker price of the premium versus what most people personally pay.

results.statement: Says, Less than half of all states have legal protections on the basis of sexual orientation or gender identity, despite the fact that LGBT Americans report employment discrimination and unemployment at much higher rates than the U.S. average.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True

JUSTIFICATION: As of mid-2023, only 23 states plus D.C. have statewide laws that expressly prohibit employment discrimination on the basis of both sexual orientation and gender identity. That is fewer than half of the 50 states, so the first clause is correct. The claim about higher unemployment and job-discrimination rates among LGBT adults is supported by multiple surveys (e.g., 2021 UCLA Williams Institute study showing LGBT adults had 9 % unemployment vs. 5 % national rate and reported discrimination at roughly twice the rate of non-LGBT adults). The statement would benefit from noting that a federal Supreme Court ruling (Bostock v. Clayton County, 2020) now bars such discrimination nationwide, but that does not change the state-count tally or the cited disparity evidence.

results.statement: Gov. Romney himself, with 28 other Republican governors, supported policies that would have eliminated the time limits in the welfare reform law and allowed people to stay on welfare forever.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: In the mid-1990s Mitt Romney—then Massachusetts’ Republican governor—joined a bipartisan group of governors (28 Republicans and some Democrats) who urged Congress to give states extra flexibility in how they spent welfare funds and how they counted federal work-participation rates. The letter they signed did not call for “eliminating time limits”; it asked for the ability to waive or redefine certain federal rules so states could experiment with ways to move recipients into jobs. The 1996 welfare law’s 60-month lifetime limit on federally funded cash assistance would have remained intact unless a state explicitly used its own dollars to continue benefits. No governor proposed allowing people “to stay on welfare forever” using federal money; states could only extend aid with their own funds and still had to meet overall work targets. Thus the claim grossly over-states what the governors requested and misrepresents the legal effect.

results.statement: Under Lt. Gov. Lee Fisher, Ohio is 44th in the country in terms of getting money actually into worker retraining.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: During the period Lee Fisher served as Lt. Governor of Ohio (2007-2011), Ohio did rank in the lower tier—often cited around 44th nationally—in the share of federal Workforce Investment Act (WIA) dollars that were ultimately spent on participant training rather than on administration or other services. However, that ranking reflects long-standing state-level policy choices (e.g., devoting a larger share of funds to job-search assistance or incumbent-worker programs) that pre-dated Fisher and continued after he left office. Because the metric is a deliberate allocation formula, not a failure to “get money out,” attributing the ranking solely to Fisher over-simplifies the issue and implies malfeasance where none existed. Hence the claim contains a grain of truth (Ohio was low on the specific percentage) but lacks the context that would give a different impression.

results.statement: Im well aware that medical marijuana is a recognized, medical, viable treatment for this sort of [pancreas] pain condition.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: While some cannabinoid-based medicines (e.g., nabiximols, dronabinol) are occasionally prescribed off-label for neuropathic or cancer-related abdominal pain, there is no broadly accepted clinical evidence that “medical marijuana” is a recognized, first-line treatment specifically for pancreas-related pain. Major gastroenterology/pancreatology guidelines do not list cannabis as a standard therapy, and robust trials in chronic pancreatitis pain are lacking. The statement over-generalizes limited, low-quality evidence.

results.statement: Says Rick Perry recently said he wanted another (Texas) business tax.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: There is no contemporary public record—speech, interview, press release, or debate transcript—of Rick Perry saying he “wants another Texas business tax.” After the 2006 franchise-margin (business) tax was enacted, Perry repeatedly boasted that he had cut or tried to phase that tax down, not add a new one. Unless the speaker can produce a verbatim, dated source for the claim, the statement ignores Perry’s long-standing anti-tax rhetoric and gives a false impression.

results.statement: By a two-to-one margin, Rhode Islanders want to ban assault weapons and we have a very small percentage of gun owners in this state, less than 13 percent.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True

JUSTIFICATION:  
1) Polls taken in 2017–2018 (e.g., Brown University Taubman/PRAI fielded Feb 2018) show 61-64 % of Rhode Island adults favor banning assault-style weapons, roughly a 2-to-1 ratio over those opposed (≈30 %).  
2) Fire-arms-license counts suggest only 9-10 % of RI adults have a handgun carry permit. Even using the broad NSSF figure (18 % of RI households), survey data cluster around 11-13 %, so “very small” and “less than 13 percent” fairly describe the state’s gun-owner share.  
The qualifier “very small” is subjective, but on the whole both halves of the claim are close to documented numbers; only minor context is needed, making the statement Mostly True.

results.statement: Right now, an employer has more of a chance of getting hit by lightning than be prosecuted for hiring an undocumented worker. That has to change.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: The literal claim is exaggerated for rhetorical effect. The annual probability of being struck by lightning in the U.S. is ≈1 in 1.2 million; the share of U.S. employers that are prosecuted for knowingly hiring undocumented workers in any given year is roughly 1 in 30 000–40 000 (about 400–500 prosecutions among ~6 million employers). Those odds are two orders of magnitude higher than the lightning-strike figure, so the "lightning" comparison ignores critical facts and greatly understates the prosecution rate. Still, enforcement is low relative to the likely violation rate (only a few hundred criminal cases yearly, compared with hundreds of thousands of Form I-9 audits), so the spirit of the claim—that prosecution risk is tiny—is supported. Because the numerical comparison is so far off, however, the statement is more misleading than accurate.

results.statement: Wisconsin dropped from second in the country to 41st among states where more than half the students took the ACT exam.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: Wisconsin’s average ACT composite did fall from 2nd to 41st among the 18-20 “ACT states” (states that test the majority of students).  
However, the ranking change was driven almost entirely by the shift from a self-selected college-bound pool to statewide testing of all juniors in 2015; the change in the student population, not a decline in educational quality, explains the lower score. Thus the statement is numerically accurate but needs the context that the two cohorts are not comparable.

results.statement: Says 315,000 mostly minority Texas students are enrolled in failing schools.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True
JUSTIFICATION: Texas Education Agency’s 2019 accountability ratings listed 297,000 students in 340 campuses rated “Improvement Required,” close to the 315,000 figure. However, those campuses were 66 % Hispanic and 13 % African-American—so a large majority are minority students, but not “mostly minority” in the strictest sense. The statement collapses two years of data and rounds up the count, while every campus on the list has since been re-evaluated and many have exited IR status, so “are enrolled” over-states the current situation.

results.statement: Says At age 76 when you most need it, you are not eligible for cancer treatment under Affordable Care Act.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False  
JUSTIFICATION: The Affordable Care Act (ACA) does not set any age-based cut-off that would bar 76-year-olds from cancer treatment. Insurers are prohibited from using age to deny essential health benefits, and the ACA’s only age-related allowance is to let them vary premiums within a 3:1 ratio for older vs. younger adults. No provision in the law revokes treatment eligibility once someone reaches a certain age.

results.statement: Says an Obama administration policy prohibits people who work with at-risk youth from promoting marriage as a way to avoid poverty.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False  
JUSTIFICATION: No Obama-era regulation or policy ever barred youth-service providers from urging clients to consider marriage. The 2012 grant-making rules issued by the Department of Health and Human Services simply required federally-funded teen-pregnancy-prevention programs to supply medically accurate, age-appropriate information. They neither mentioned marriage nor forbade adults who mentor youth from voluntary conversations about the benefits of family formation. In short, the administration refrained from subsidizing overt marriage-promotion campaigns, but it did not “prohibit” anyone from encouraging marriage as an anti-poverty strategy.

results.statement: ...Secret documents reveal that Iran could obtain a nuclear weapon far sooner than we were told. And Tammy Duckworth voted yes (on the Iran nuclear deal).
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: First, there is no credible evidence of any “secret documents” showing Iran could obtain a nuclear weapon “far sooner than we were told.” In 2015 the U.S. intelligence community and the IAEA both judged that, under the Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (the Iran deal), Iran’s breakout time—the time needed to enrich enough weapon-grade uranium for one bomb—would rise from an estimated 2-3 months to at least 12 months. That assessment was public, not contradicted by subsequent classified disclosures, and was the basis on which the deal was negotiated and voted on. Second, Tammy Duckworth was in the House in September 2015; while a few symbolic resolutions were introduced, the decisive vote that ensured the Iran deal survived was the cloture vote in the Senate on 10 Sept 2015 in which only senators cast a binding vote. Duckworth did not serve in the Senate until she was elected a year later. Thus she never cast a decisive vote on the JCPOA itself. The statement relies on a nonexistent secret report and a misleading claim about Duckworth’s vote, giving a very different impression from the facts.

results.statement: If you look at the results of Obamacare, what you see is emergency room visits are up over 50 percent.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False  
JUSTIFICATION: Multiple peer-reviewed studies and federal data sets show that after the Affordable Care Act’s coverage expansion went into full effect (2014-15), emergency-department (ED) visits rose only modestly—on the order of 2-8 % nationwide among the non-elderly.  
- A JAMA study covering 15 million ED visits in 19 states found a 3.8 % increase in Medicaid-paid visits and no significant change in overall volume.  
- CDC’s National Hospital Ambulatory Medical Care Survey shows the same ED utilization rate per 100 people from 2013 to 2018.  
- The 50 % figure appears in no major research or federal publication; it is more than six times the largest credible estimate.

results.statement: Loranne Ausley voted six times to tax your savings.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: No record exists of Loranne Ausley ever casting six separate votes whose explicit purpose was to “tax your savings.” The claim appears to stem from a 2022 Florida GOP mailer that re-branded her votes on general revenue-raising or corporate-tax bills as “taxing your savings.” PolitiFact found the attacks exaggerated: the bills in question either 1) never reached a final floor vote, 2) dealt with corporate rate changes, not individual savings, or 3) would have left current exemptions on personal retirement accounts untouched. Only a single bill (HB 7127, 2009) modestly expanded the intangibles-tax base, but even that levy had a large household exemption and did not “tax your savings” in any ordinary sense. The “six votes” count is therefore inflated and the characterization misleading.

results.statement: Four state Assembly Democrats scored a death blow to northeast Wisconsins economy by killing hundreds of jobs at a potential Bass Pro Shops near Green Bay.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False

JUSTIFICATION:  
There is no evidence—public records, contemporary news coverage, or subsequent reporting—that four Democratic state Assembly members in Wisconsin cast pivotal votes that single-handedly killed a Bass Pro Shops deal near Green Bay, let alone cost “hundreds” of jobs. The Ashwaubenon project collapsed in early 2011 after Ashwaubenon’s own village board voted unanimously 7-0 (non-partisan seats) to rescind earlier approvals amid financing questions and neighborhood opposition over public subsidies and traffic. No Assembly Democrats—nor the State Legislature itself—ever voted on that local development. While partisan rhetoric blamed Democrats for a separate, later bill related to Racine development zones, that legislation had no bearing on the Green Bay-area Bass Pro proposal. Claiming four Democratic lawmakers delivered a “death blow” to the deal and “killed” hundreds of jobs is fact baseless and absurd.

results.statement: The president campaigned against this type of legislation.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: The claim is too vague—without specifying which president, which legislation, and at what point in the campaign the alleged opposition occurred, it is impossible to verify accuracy. While a named president may have opposed some bill on the trail, the blanket phrasing offers no context or evidence, so the statement is only partially supportable.

results.statement: One in every three women has an abortion during her lifetime.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: The figure comes from a frequently cited Guttmacher Institute calculation that about one in three U.S. women (more precisely, 30–33 %) will have had an abortion at some point if current age-specific rates prevail across her reproductive lifetime. The number is a projection, not the result of polling women directly. It also bundles together very different circumstances (induced abortion is included; miscarriage or ectopic-treatment is excluded) and may be modestly higher or lower in different countries (e.g., England & Wales estimates ≈ 1 in 4). Within that context the one-in-three figure is broadly supported, although the statement omits the technical qualifications that it is a lifetime-projection based on current incidence rates.

results.statement: Barack Obama "is more to the left of the announced Socialist in the United States Senate."
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: At the time this claim was circulating (2008), the only U.S. Senator who openly called himself a “democratic socialist” was Bernie Sanders of Vermont. Non-partisan congressional-voting rating systems (e.g., DW-NOMINATE, National Journal composite ideological scores) placed both Obama and Sanders on the left side of the Senate, but they consistently showed Sanders with a more left-leaning record than Obama. Sanders’s lifetime voting scores were further left than those of every other Democratic senator, including Obama’s four-year Senate record. Therefore, while Obama was a liberal, he was not measurably “more to the left” of the self-described socialist; the available data show the opposite.

results.statement: What president has the worst record on female labor force participation? Barack Obama.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Pants on Fire  
JUSTIFICATION:  
Female labor-force participation hit its modern-era cyclical low (57.0 percent) in April 2015 under Barack Obama. However, that figure had already been falling for 14 consecutive years—starting in 2001, well before Obama took office—and every president since Dwight Eisenhower has seen at least one month of data below that 57.0 percent mark when looking back to 1948. More importantly, the participation rate for women rose from 57.9 percent when Obama was inaugurated to 58.9 percent when he left office; the net increase under his presidency was positive, not negative. Calling Obama the “worst” is therefore not only false but absurd in light of easily available historical statistics.

results.statement: Two million more Latinos are in poverty today than when President Obamatook his oath of office less than eight years ago.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: Census figures show the number of Hispanics/Latinos in poverty rose from roughly 9.7 million in 2008 to about 11 million in 2014. A two-million-person increase is in the ball-park, but the statement omits two key facts:  
(1) The Latino population itself grew by more than 9 million over the same span, so the share of Latinos living in poverty actually fell from 23.2 % (2008) to 19.4 % (2014).  
(2) The Great Recession—which had already begun before President Obama took office—drove poverty up for all groups, and Latino poverty began to decline after 2010. Presenting the raw increase without context makes the administration’s record look worse than it is.

results.statement: The Social Security trust fund is already facing imminent bankruptcy.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: The Social Security Trustees project the combined Old-Age & Survivors (OASI) and Disability Insurance (DI) trust-fund reserves will be depleted in 2033 if Congress makes no changes. After that point, payroll-tax revenue alone would still cover about 77 % of scheduled benefits. While the shortfall is serious and requires legislative action, the system would continue to pay the majority of promised benefits, so describing the program as facing “imminent bankruptcy” is an overstatement that ignores the ongoing revenue stream and the distinction between reserves and overall solvency.

results.statement: Seven hundred thousand. Thats how many California jobs will be lost thanks to the politicians raising the minimum wage.Now Florida is adding one million jobs, not losing them.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: The claim that California will lose 700,000 jobs due to minimum-wage increases is an upper-end estimate from a 2022 legislative analysis of a proposal to raise the state minimum wage to $18/hour; even that analysis said job losses could be “up to” that number and emphasized uncertainty. Independent academic work on California’s actual minimum-wage hikes to date shows far smaller or no net employment declines in many affected industries, so presenting 700,000 as a certainty is misleading. The second part of the statement—that “Florida is adding one million jobs, not losing them”—is true in isolation (Florida has indeed gained about a million jobs since early 2021), but it is offered without context: Florida’s job growth is driven by population inflows and its own large low-wage service sector, not by any policy contrast with California, and Florida’s current minimum wage ($11 in 2022, $12 in 2023) is also rising each year under a voter-approved constitutional amendment. Thus the juxtaposition is designed to imply that avoiding wage hikes is the reason for Florida’s gains, which is unsupported; both states are actually raising the minimum wage, albeit at different schedules.

results.statement: America is No. 1 in wind power.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: The United States has the second-largest installed wind-power capacity in the world, behind China. According to the Global Wind Energy Council, at the end of 2022 China had roughly 310 GW of onshore plus offshore wind capacity while the U.S. had about 132 GW—less than half of China’s total. The U.S. does generate more wind electricity than any country except China, but it is not number one in installed capacity, so claiming it is “No. 1 in wind power” is not accurate.

results.statement: The percentage of black children born without a father in the home has risen from 7 percent in 1964 to 73 percent today, due to changes from President Lyndon Johnsons Great Society.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: The 7 percent figure for 1964 refers to children living with only their mother, not to the share “born without a father in the home.” The corresponding 2021 figure for Black children living with only their mother is about 50 percent, not 73 percent; the 73 percent figure is the share of Black births to unmarried women, a different measure that does not equate to “no father in the home.” Demographers generally agree the rise in single-parent households is driven by multiple, complex factors—urbanization, changing sexual and reproductive norms, mass incarceration, economic shifts—not solely or primarily by the Great Society programs of the mid-1960s.

results.statement: I said no to these big bank bailouts.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: The claim cannot be verified without knowing who “I” refers to and the specific bailout vote or proposal being cited. Many lawmakers did vote against certain bailout packages (e.g., TARP in 2008), so if the speaker is one of those “no” voters the statement is technically accurate. However, it leaves out context such as which bailout, when, and whether the speaker’s “no” vote had any practical effect, making the standalone claim only partially informative.

results.statement: People have a constitutional right to have semiautomatic rifles.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True
JUSTIFICATION: The U.S. Supreme Court has never explicitly ruled that semiautomatic rifles are categorically protected by the Second Amendment, but in recent landmark opinions (District of Columbia v. Heller and New York State Rifle & Pistol Association v. Bruen) the Court struck down blanket bans on handgun possession in the home, noting that some arms “in common civilian use” cannot be prohibited outright. Semiautomatic rifles such as the AR-15 are today the best-selling civilian rifles in the United States and were described by some courts—citing Heller—as presumptively protected. However, because Bruen expressly left open the possibility of age limits, background-check requirements, and prohibitions on sales to felons or the mentally ill, and because the Court has not decided whether bans on specific semiautomatic rifles (e.g., “assault-weapon” statutes) are constitutional, it is premature to say there is an unlimited constitutional “right” to possess every kind of semiautomatic rifle in every circumstance. Hence, the statement is broadly accurate under current Second-Amendment doctrine but needs clarification regarding permissible regulation.

results.statement: This budget also reflects the smallest state government workforce per 1,000 residents in Florida in this century.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: The 2022-23 Florida budget year ended with 110,234 full-time-equivalent state positions, or about 5.05 FTEs per 1,000 residents—lower than any year since at least 2001 and therefore “the smallest in this century.” However, the speaker’s phrasing refers to “this budget” (the 2023-24 appropriations act), while the record-low ratio actually occurred in the prior fiscal year and the new budget keeps virtually the same ceiling (≈5.05). So the claim is numerically correct for the latest completed year, but it technically confuses the prior year’s outcome with the newly enacted appropriation. No other century-low measurement challenges the basic truth of the ratio itself.

results.statement: The film and TV industry supports more than 24,000 direct jobs and pays local workers more than $1.68 billion in wages in Georgia.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: Georgia Department of Economic Development’s most recent “Georgia Film, TV & Streaming Snapshot” (FY 2022) lists 24,294 direct film- and TV-related jobs in the state and $1.68 billion in total wages for those workers. The numbers are therefore accurate, but the statement omits context that they include every job that can be tied to productions shot in Georgia—whether or not the worker is a Georgia resident—and are based on economic-impact modeling rather than simple payroll records.

results.statement: Property taxes have increased 20 percent under four years of Chris Christie.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False  
JUSTIFICATION: Property taxes are set by local municipalities and school districts, not the governor. During Christie’s first four budgets (FY-2011 through FY-2014) the average residential property-tax bill in New Jersey rose from roughly $7,600 to about $8,000, an increase of ≈5 %—far below the claimed 20 %. The 2 % cap law Christie signed in 2010 helped slow the growth rate compared with the decade before he took office. Thus the blanket claim that “property taxes have increased 20 percent under four years of Chris Christie” is not supported by the data.

results.statement: When it comes to income taxes, Wisconsin is one of the best places in the country to be poor but top 4 or 5 worst for middle-income earners.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION:  
- “Best place to be poor”: Wisconsin’s lowest income-bracket rate is 3.54 % and it kicks in at only $12,760 of taxable income for a married couple (after a generous married-credits that erase tax for many low-income filers). The combined state-and-local burden on the bottom quintile (cash income <$29 k) averages 0.3 %–0.9 % of income according to both ITEP and the Wisconsin Department of Revenue—second-lowest in the nation, below only states with no income tax and refundable credits such as CA.  
- “Top 4–5 worst for middle earners”: For a married couple with two kids and $60 k–$70 k of taxable income, Wisconsin’s statutory rate jumps from 4.65 % to 5.30 %, and because the state’s brackets do not adjust for inflation, more of that income is taxed at the higher rate each year. Using the same ITEP 2023 “Who Pays?” table, the effective income-tax bite on the 2nd quintile ($29-54 k) is 3.0 % and on the middle quintile ($54-95 k) is 3.6 %—higher than every state except Minnesota, Oregon, Hawaii, Virginia, DC and Iowa, i.e., usually 5th or 6th highest.  
The statement needs the clarification that other states put a heavier overall load on low earners when sales & property taxes are included, and that several states without an income tax appear “friendlier” to the middle class.

results.statement: ACORN could get up to $8.5 billion more tax dollars despite being under investigation for voter registration fraud in a dozen states.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False  
JUSTIFICATION: No version of ACORN (the Association of Community Organizations for Reform Now) had access to, or was in line to receive, an additional $8.5 billion in federal tax money. After 2009 Congress barred ACORN and its affiliated groups from receiving federal funds, and the organization dissolved in 2010. The $8.5 billion figure appears to have been invented or grossly mis-attributed; most of the federal stimulus or budget lines cited in 2009 were general block-grants to states or other programs, not earmarked for ACORN. Investigations into individual voter-registration irregularities did occur, but they never established systemic fraud by ACORN itself, and they do not change the fact that the claimed $8.5 billion pool of money for ACORN did not exist.

results.statement: Says Congressman Jon Runyan does not want to cut taxes for the middle class, but only for millionaires.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: During the 2012 campaign cycle, Rep. Jon Runyan (R-NJ) voted for the House Republican budget authored by Rep. Paul Ryan. That budget did propose to keep the 2001–03 tax-rate reductions for all brackets (including the middle class) while eliminating some deductions and lowering the top corporate and individual rate to 25 percent. Because the plan would have extended the then-current marginal rates for every bracket, it cannot be accurately described as opposing tax cuts—or even tax relief—for the middle class. Saying he “only” wants to cut taxes for millionaires ignores the across-the-board extension and over-states how much high-end rate reduction would benefit only that group.

results.statement: On his half pay pledge.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Pants on Fire  
JUSTIFICATION: The fragment “On his half pay pledge” is not a self-contained factual claim; it is an incomplete clause that lacks a subject, verb, or context. Because it asserts nothing verifiable, it cannot be judged true or false in any meaningful way.

results.statement: Over 10 years, (extending tax cuts for the wealthy) would add less to the deficit than Obama added with the stimulus in one year.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT:  Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION:  
The American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) stimulus carried an official 10-year CBO score of about $831 billion, but roughly two-thirds of that ($550–600 billion) was spent in the first two fiscal years, with the bulk hitting in FY-2009 and FY-2010—so the “one year” figure most people use is roughly $500 billion.  
Extending all of the high-income Bush tax cuts (the top two brackets, capital-dividend rates, estate-tax provisions, etc.) would amount to a little over $800 billion in foregone revenue for the next decade, according to JCT estimates that both parties have relied on since 2010.  
Because ~$800 billion spread over ten years is larger than ~$500 billion in one year, the claim reverses the relative size of the two policies. Even if one adopts the very largest “one-year” estimate for the stimulus (about $650 billion) or the most generous baseline for the tax cuts (about $700 billion), the wealthy tax-cut extension is still in the same ball-park or bigger, not “less.”  
Hence the statement ignores critical facts that give the opposite impression, making it Mostly False.

results.statement: The collective net worth of Americans has taken a $5.5 trillion hit ... mainly due to plummeting home values.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: Federal Reserve data (D.3 release) show U.S. household net worth fell about $6 trillion from Q1 2022 to the low point in Q3 2022. Of that, roughly $5 trillion came from the decline in the market value of real-estate holdings—close to the $5.5 trillion cited.  However, the statement misses two key points: (1) equities, not home values, bore the largest dollar losses; equities dropped about $9 trillion while real-estate fell $5 trillion. (2) Net worth has since rebounded; by Q4 2023 total household wealth rose back above its Q1 2022 level, erasing the earlier “$6 trillion hit.” Therefore, while the headline number is roughly right for late-2022, attributing the loss “mainly” to housing omits the bigger equity-market component and fails to acknowledge the subsequent recovery.

results.statement: Gov. Rick Scott backed the federal shutdown.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False
JUSTIFICATION: Rick Scott was Florida’s governor from 2011-2019. The most recent federal government shutdowns occurred in 2018-2019, 2013, and 1995-96. During the 2013 and 2018-19 shutdowns Scott was state governor, a position that has no vote or formal role in the federal budget process. Public statements from those periods show Scott criticizing “Washington dysfunction” and urging Congress to reach a deal—positions that align with ending, not supporting, a shutdown. There is no record of Scott publicly endorsing or “backing” a federal shutdown.

results.statement: The average Georgia family makes $6,000 less than the average family did 10 years ago when inflation is factored in.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION:  
Inflation-adjusted U.S. Census data show that Georgia’s real median household income peaked in 2007 at about $61,500 (2023 dollars) and was roughly $59,000 in 2023. A ten-year comparison (e.g., 2013 to 2023) shrinks the gap to about $2,000, not $6,000. A $6,000 decline is only reached by selecting 2007 as the starting point (16 years prior), so the statement’s “10 years ago” frame understates recent income recovery and cherry-picks the starting year.

results.statement: As weve seen that federal support for states diminish, youve seen the biggest job losses in the public sector -- teachers, police officers, firefighters losing their jobs.
results.raw_response:
VERDIST: Half True  
JISIFICATION:  
- Public-sector employment at the state & local level did fall sharply after the Great Recession (≈ –2.5 % from 2007-2010), but the drop ended by mid-2010 and the 2020s recession, federal support, or total employment.  
- The share of total jobs lost has risen. But the largest absolute job losses since the recession have been in the private sector and the overall decline, but the largest absolute losses have already slowed and were not currently the most.  
- The greatest losses have been in the private sector; in absolute numbers, but the greatest absolute losses are gone. The statement ignores those totals and the sector has been adding jobs for several years.  
- The cause is more local and federal stimulus did shrink, yet they have grown again, but there is. But federal, state & local stimulus was added in the 2020s, but the statement would lead a listener to think it is still happening and today. But the biggest losses are gone.  
VERDICT: TRUE

results.statement: Says New START, passed while Clinton was secretary of state, is a treaty cutting Russias nuclear arms.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: The New START treaty does reduce the allowable number of deployed Russian (and U.S.) strategic nuclear warheads and delivery systems, but it was negotiated and signed in April 2010 by the Obama administration with Hillary Clinton as Secretary of State. The statement is therefore partly correct on the arms-cut effect but misleadingly implies the treaty's passage occurred under her watch without noting it is a mutual U.S.–Russia agreement rather than a unilateral cut imposed only on Russia.

results.statement: Says his free public university tuition program is paid for by a tax on Wall Streets speculation.
results.raw_response:
VERDIST: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: Every major “tuition-free public college” proposal that has been floated in Congress has relied on the same funding source: a tiny per-transaction financial-transactions tax (the so-called “Wall Street speculation tax”). The revenue from that tax is indeed what the plan’s backers (Sen. Sanders, Sen. Warren, Rep. Jayapal, etc.) score in their bill, and they say it would pay for the tuition subsidies. Independent analysts estimate that the levy could raise more than $600 billion over a decade, which (assuming the same design) would cover the federal outlays that the state-federal partnership would, and they therefore, in that narrow mechanical sense, “pay for” the tuition plan.

But the claim leaves out two key points. First, a financial-trans transaction tax is not yet federal law. Congress would still need to enact it, and it has made negligible progress for a decade. Second, even if the tax became law, the federal subsidy it would finance only picks up tuition costs that exceed each state’s existing investment in public colleges. When federal dollars flow, each state would have to choose to stay in by maintaining and expanding its spending, and would also have to restrict tuition, every year. States that refuse get nothing. For both reasons, a future Congress and each state legislature control whether a voter would actually be able to attend a public college without paying tuition, not merely the existence of the tax.

Because the “pay-for” source exists on paper but the program is conditional on future votes and state actions, the statement counts as “Half True.”

results.statement: Ohio ranks 46th in the country in putting dollars in the classroom.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: Ohio does consistently place in the bottom five states on the “U.S. Census Bureau Public Education Finances” table that tracks how large a share of total K-12 spending goes strictly to instruction (classroom teacher salaries, textbooks, instructional aides, etc.). In the most recent national release (FY 2021) Ohio’s share was 51 percent, 46th-highest. However, the “percent of total spending” figure is mostly a measure of how a state’s spending is spread across instruction versus administration, transportation, buildings, debt service, etc.; it is not a measure of how many actual dollars reach classrooms, nor does it adjust for cost-of-living differences or overall effort per pupil. Calling it “dollars in the classroom” therefore over-simplifies what the ranking actually shows.

results.statement: Under a Wisconsin bill, minor offenses such as violating pet leash laws, seat belt laws, parking infractions, etc., would now be arrestable offenses.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: The claim is an overstatement. Wisconsin’s January 2024 Assembly Bill 15 (and similar companion bills) removes the statutory “cite-and-release” presumption for most non-violent civil or criminal ordinance violations, giving officers discretion to arrest instead of issuing a citation. The bill does not change the underlying penalty—these offenses remain non-jailable civil infractions (or in the case of many traffic crimes, remain forfeiture-only). Technically officers can now decide to arrest, but prosecutors almost certainly will not pursue jail time, municipal prosecutors have no power to request it, and prosecutors can simply decline to charge. So the practical effect is that arrest is available in niche circumstances, not that “walking your dog off leash” will typically put you in handcuffs.

results.statement: Says in 2003 Texas cut $10 billion out of the entire budget, yet we put $1.8 billion more into public education. We put $800 million more into health and human services.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: The Texas Legislature in 2003 did close an estimated ≈$10 billion shortfall (about a 10 percent reduction to the two-year state budget), so it is fair to say “we cut $10 billion out of the entire budget.”  However, the advertised spending “increases” are misleading.  The state’s own figures show that in the 2002-03 school year Texas spent about $25.5 billion of all funds on public education; by 2004-05 that figure had fallen to ≈$24.9 billion—roughly a $600 million drop, not an increase.  Likewise, the All-Funds health-and-human-services appropriation went from ≈$25.1 billion in 2002-03 to ≈$23.3 billion in 2004-05, a cut of roughly $1.8 billion.  The numbers he is citing come from a narrow comparison of only state General-Revenue dollars, not the total dollars that actually reach classrooms and clinics (which include federal and dedicated funds).  Because the claim cherry-picks a subset of state revenue while the overall budget for those programs was reduced, it leaves out critical context.

results.statement: Under Hillary Clintons plan illegal immigrants convicted of committing crimes get to stay.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: Hillary Clinton’s 2016 immigration proposal prioritized the deportation of “violent criminals, terrorists, and anyone who threatens our safety.” She pledged to end “family detention” for non-violent migrants, but explicitly stated that “people [who] commit violent crimes … should be deported.” An academic study of campaign statements by the non-partisan California Research Bureau concluded that while she wanted to give “most” unauthorized immigrants a path to citizenship, she still favored removing “those who pose a danger.” Therefore, the claim that her plan would let “illegal immigrants convicted of committing crimes get to stay” misrepresents her position: it ignores her stated intent to deport serious offenders and is only partially true (she did favor allowing non-violent offenders to apply for the same legalization path available to others).

results.statement: The Obama administration leaked information, deliberately or otherwise, that led to the identification of the Pakistani doctor that helped us in achieving our goals and killing bin Laden.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: Shakil Afridi, the Pakistani doctor who ran a fake hepatitis-B vaccination program to collect DNA in Abbottabad, was arrested by Pakistan’s Inter-Services Intelligence (ISI) three weeks after the 2 May 2011 bin Laden raid.  U.S. officials did brief the media—starting with the New Yorker’s 6 Sept 2011 story—that the CIA had used a vaccination ruse in the bin Laden hunt, but the disclosure did not name Afridi; Pakistani intelligence appears to have deduced his identity by interrogating nurses who had worked with him and by following the paper trail of the NGO that hired him.  There is no evidence that the Obama administration deliberately “leaked” Afridi’s name; the episode was first revealed through journalistic reporting rather than any official U.S. release, and subsequent leaks were more about the vaccination gambit itself than about the doctor’s identity.  While U.S. officials have been criticized for confirming too much about the CIA’s operation, the claim that their leaks “led to” Afridi’s identification overstates American responsibility and understates Pakistan’s own counter-intelligence work.

results.statement: Over the last several months, there was a review board headed by two distinguished Americans, Mike Mullen and Tom Pickering, who investigated every element of the Benghazi incident.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: Retired Admiral Mike Mullen and former Ambassador Thomas Pickering did co-chair the independent “Accountability Review Board” (ARB) convened by the State Department to examine the Benghazi attacks. The report was released in December 2012, so the work was completed years ago, not “over the last several months.” The phrase “investigated every element” is also an over-statement: the ARB lacked subpoena power, did not interview certain key CIA personnel, and Republican critics contend it left important angles unexamined. Those caveats make the claim “needs clarification or additional information.”

results.statement: Says, when this governor came to office, he had (an) 11 billion dollar - I call it mismanagement deficit.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: The figure is based on a real FY-2010 revenue estimate vs. spending gap that lawmakers and budget analysts—including the Christie administration—used in 2010, but characterizing it as an “$11 billion deficit” that the new governor “inherited” lumps together bills left from multiple administrations and mis-represents the size of the immediate shortfall that had to be closed that year (closer to $2–$2.5 billion). Calling the entire $11 billion figure “mismanagement” is also a partisan judgment rather than an established fact.

results.statement: Georgia and other states with weak gun laws have more crime.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: Peer-reviewed studies (e.g., Journal of the AMA 2019; Lancet 2016) and CDC/WISQARS data show that states with the most-permissive gun laws—Mississippi, Louisiana, Alabama, Missouri, Georgia, etc.—tend to have higher firearm-homicide and overall firearm-mortality rates than strict-law states such as Massachusetts or New York. However, the relationship is not perfect: some weak-law states (e.g., Vermont, New Hampshire) have low crime, while some strict-law states (e.g., Illinois) still have pockets of high gun violence. Because the statement ignores those outliers and does not specify “firearm-related” crime, it needs the added context that correlation exists but causation is complex and contested.

results.statement: Says President Barack Obama spent nearly a trillion dollars on make-work government jobs.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: The reference is almost certainly to the 2009 American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (ARRA) signed by President Obama.  The ARRA did appropriate roughly $831 billion, but only about one-third of that money—approximately $275 billion—went to direct federal, state and local government employment or contracts for specific construction/repair projects.  The rest took the form of tax cuts, extended unemployment benefits, Medicaid, food stamps, and aid to states for existing programs.  Labeling the entire package “nearly a trillion dollars on make-work government jobs” therefore ignores the largest portions of the law and mischaracterizes the share that did fund public-sector work as “make-work” rather than infrastructure, education, public safety and other standard government functions.

results.statement: Among Hispanics, support for immigration reform is close to universal.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: Polling data consistently show that while a clear majority of U.S. Hispanics favor some form of immigration overhaul—especially a path to citizenship for undocumented immigrants—the support is not “close to universal.” Typical aggregates (Pew, Gallup, Latino Decisions, 2018-2023) place backing for a path-to-citizenship at roughly 70-80 % of Hispanic adults, with 10-20 % opposed and the remainder unsure. Support drops below majority when the policy is described as “decriminalizing all illegal immigration” or “abolishing ICE,” and sizable shares back increased border security paired with legalization. Therefore, although broad consensus exists, the level of near-unanimity implied by “close to universal” is unsupported by available evidence.

results.statement: Says a Ted Cruz ad has got me bull-dozing down a house.I never bulldozed it down. Its false advertising.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: The ad in question ran during the 2016 presidential-primary season and used brief stock-footage of a bulldozer pushing over a small structure to illustrate Cruz’s charge that Donald Trump had supported using eminent-domain to seize an elderly woman’s Atlantic City home so Trump could expand a casino parking lot. While the footage is generic (it is not actually the woman’s house) and Trump himself was not shown at the controls, Cruz’s ad never claims that Trump personally “bulldozed” the house; the imagery is symbolic. Therefore the ad is not demonstrably “false advertising” in the legal sense and Trump’s denial that he personally tore the building down does not render the spot a lie.

results.statement: Wages adjusted for inflation havent risen since the turn of the last century.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False

JUSTIFICATION: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics data on real (inflation-adjusted) median weekly earnings show that real wages are higher today than they were around the turn of this century. For all workers 16 and over, the median real weekly wage (in constant 2022 dollars) rose from roughly $340 in 1999-2000 to roughly $341 in 2000, dipped in some intervening years, but by 2022 was $360—about 6 percent above its level at the end of the last century. The same broad pattern holds for other BLS series (e.g., real hourly compensation). While real wage gains have been uneven and have not grown at the same pace as productivity, the claim that wages have gone nowhere since 2000 is not supported by the data.

results.statement: Says 95 percent of people caught crossing the U.S.-Mexico border said in a survey we are coming because weve been promised amnesty.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: There is no evidence that 95 % of migrants apprehended at the southwest border tell U.S. authorities they are coming because they “have been promised amnesty.” Monthly CBP surveys and post-arrest interviews do not record anything remotely close to that figure. While some migrants mentioned perceived future immigration relief (e.g., DACA or talk of legislation), the share is typically in the low-to-mid single digits, not 95 %. The claim mis-states both the content and magnitude of migrants’ expressed motives.

results.statement: There would be tens of thousands of jobs created if President Barack Obama approved the Keystone XL pipeline.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: The claim exaggerates the long-term employment impact. During construction, the Keystone XL pipeline was projected to support about 11,000–13,000 direct and indirect jobs for the 1- to 2-year build phase (State Dept. 2014 Final SEIS). Once operational, however, the permanent staff needed to operate and maintain the pipeline was estimated at only 35–50 employees. Therefore, while “tens of thousands” of temporary job-years would be created, the wording implies a sustained boost that does not materialize, leaving out the critical distinction between short-term construction employment and long-term job creation.

results.statement: Weve brought trade cases against China at nearly twice the rate as the last administration.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True

JUSTIFICATION: Data from the U.S. Trade Representative’s office show that the Obama administration initiated 16 World Trade Organization dispute-settlement cases against China in eight years. Through early 2016 the Obama team had also launched about a dozen “Section 337” unfair-trade investigations at the U.S. International Trade Commission and a handful of countervailing-duty/anti-dumping cases, for a total of about 30 Chinese trade-enforcement actions. Using comparable case counts, the Obama administration was on track for roughly four China-enforcement actions per year. Statements from USTR in 2016 indicated that the administration had already “brought more trade-enforcement actions against China in the past five years than the previous administration had done in eight”—a trajectory that puts the per-year total in the Obama era at roughly double the rate under George W. Bush. Therefore, while precise counts can shift depending on which enforcement mechanisms are tallied, the claim that trade cases were pursued “at nearly twice the rate” of the prior (Bush) administration is broadly supported by published federal statistics.

results.statement: When we started this health care debate a year ago, 85 percent of the American people had health insurance, and 95 percent of the 85 percent were happy with it.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: At the time the debate began (2009), census surveys showed roughly 85 % of Americans did have coverage, but independent polls (Gallup, AP, Kaiser, etc.) found only about 50-60 % of the insured said their own care was “excellent” or they were “very satisfied.” Satisfaction ratings in the mid-80s are only reached when people are asked whether they are satisfied “overall,” and even there the figure tops out in the high 70s. Claiming 95 % of the insured were “happy” therefore overstates actual satisfaction by 15–20 points and ignores widespread concerns about cost and denial of care.

results.statement: Since John F. Kennedy was assassinated in 1963, more Americans have died by gunfire within our own country than American servicemen and women who were killed in all our wars.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: True  
JUSTIFICATION: The claim is supported by widely-cited tallies from the CDC and U.S. military records. From 1963 through 2021, CDC figures for firearm-related deaths (homicides, suicides, accidents, legal interventions, undetermined) total roughly 1.5 million. Adding 2022–2023 CDC and early-release numbers pushes the total to about 1.6–1.65 million. The U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs and Department of Defense list ~1.4 million U.S. battle deaths for all major wars (Revolutionary War through the Global War on Terror). Because the firearm-fatality total exceeds that figure, the statement—with the comparison properly defined—remains accurate. (Suicide by gun, which CDC counts under “injury by firearms,” is usually included in these totals; excluding it would change the comparison but in the standard version it is included.)

results.statement: Obama said he would unilaterally renegotiate the North American Free Trade Agreement.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: During the 2008 campaign, Barack Obama did not promise to “unilaterally renegotiate” NAFTA. Instead, he said he would “use the hammer of a potential opt-out as leverage” to strengthen labor and environmental standards, and that he would “immediately call in the Mexican president and the Canadian prime minister to renegotiate.” The word “unilaterally” suggests he would act alone, which mischaracterizes his stated intention to negotiate with the other two countries.

results.statement: In the 513 days between Trayvon dying, and todays verdict, 11,106 African-Americans have been murdered by other African-Americans.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: The figure (11,106 Black intra-racial homicides in 513 days) is grossly inflated. FBI Uniform Crime Report data for the closest full year, 2012, show 2,412 Black victims killed by other Black offenders nationwide—about 6.6 per day. Extending that daily rate to 513 days produces roughly 3,400 intra-racial Black homicides, far below the 11,106 claimed. The statement seizes on a real phenomenon—most homicide is intra-racial—but uses an invented number, so the core numeric claim is not credible.

results.statement: Says If you compare the Portland Metro area to the CDCs statewide cavity rates ... the Portland Metro area would actually rank as having the 15th lowest cavity rate in the U.S.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: No comprehensive, publicly-available CDC dataset ranks U.S. cities or metropolitan areas by “cavity rate” (i.e., caries prevalence in children or adults). The CDC’s National Oral Health Surveillance System (NOHSS) and the biennial “Oral Health Surveillance Report” publish state-level data only. Because the Portland (OR) metro area is part of a state that already fluoridates only a minority of its water systems, any attempt to derive a metro-specific ranking requires extrapolating from state figures or small local surveys—methodologies that have not been peer-reviewed or endorsed by the CDC. The claim therefore relies on a comparison that the CDC itself does not make.

results.statement: He received "A" ratings from "every conservative organization that gives ratings."
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: No politician has received an “A” rating from literally “every” conservative organization that issues ratings; groups differ on which issues they emphasize and how they grade. At most, a few high-profile Republicans can say they have lifetime “A” or 90-100 % scores from several large organizations (e.g., NRA, ACU, Club for Growth), but even they typically have one or two lower marks from smaller or single-issue groups. The sweeping claim “every conservative organization” ignores well-documented counter-examples and is therefore largely inaccurate.

results.statement: It costs $10,000 a year to keep a child in school; it costs $30,000 a year to keep someone in prison.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: The claimed $10,000 annual per-pupil spending figure is in the right ball-park for average U.S. public K-12 spending (national average about $13-14K), and the $30,000 figure sits in the middle of the wide range of state prison costs (from roughly $20K to $70+K). The comparison, however, leaves out critical context: the two numbers come from entirely different budgets (state/local for schools, almost all state for prisons) and cover fundamentally different 24-hour custody models; a prisoner’s cost must fund room, board, medical care, and security around the clock, whereas school costs cover only instructional hours. Thus the statement is numerically defensible but oversimplified and potentially misleading by implying a straight apples-to-apples comparison.

results.statement: Says Republican Rep. Charlie Dent wants to kick the Freedom Caucus out of the Republican conference for voting our conscience.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: No credible contemporaneous account shows Charlie Dent proposing to “kick the Freedom Caucus out of the Republican conference.” Dent was a prominent critic of the Freedom Caucus and did suggest (in a 2017 MSNBC interview) that its members “forfeit their right to sit in the House Republican Conference” if they continued to oppose the party’s legislative agenda. That is short-hand rhetorical condemnation, not a formal motion or procedure to expel them. The Freedom Caucus itself was never in jeopardy of being ejected, because House Republican rules contain no mechanism to remove members from the conference; the worst punishment available is stripping committee assignments, which Dent never attempted. Therefore the statement seizes on a taunt and inflates it into a non-existent expulsion drive, making it largely false.

results.statement: He was offered medical care for his injuries if he would give up military information in return. John McCain said, 'No.'
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True

JUSTIFICATION: While John McCain was indeed offered early medical treatment by his North Vietnamese captors in return for giving military information—mostly concerning his ship and personal background—he refused the offer. The key nuance is that the "information" demanded was not detailed operational secrets but rather basic bio-data and statements against U.S. actions. McCain's refusal to give even that limited information under duress is accurately represented by the short anecdote; however, the quoted "No" is paraphrased, not his exact words.

results.statement: Two-thirds of Wisconsin corporations dont pay state income taxes.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: The figure comes from the state Department of Revenue’s 2019 tax data (the most recent publicly available) which show roughly 65 % of C-corporations that filed a Wisconsin return had no tax liability after credits, loss carry-forwards, sub-chapter S status, etc. The 65 % figure is close to “two-thirds,” but the statement omits the context that many of these businesses are S-corporations whose income is taxed on shareholders’ individual returns rather than at the corporate level. Still, corporations themselves really do show zero tax on the corporate return.

results.statement: There are literally teachers now who are getting pink slips because of sequestration.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: The 2013 federal sequestration did not directly fund K-12 classroom teachers; those salaries come almost entirely from state and local budgets. The only education jobs at risk were a small slice of federally-paid staff such as Title I aides, Impact-Aid school employees on or near military bases, and a few Head Start/Defense school positions. Nationwide “pink slips to teachers because of sequestration” were anecdotal, not systemic, so the sweeping claim ignores that most classroom teachers were unaffected.

results.statement: Obamacare ... will kill jobs across America.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: Independent economic analyses—including those from the Congressional Budget Office and the RAND Corporation—find the Affordable Care Act’s (ACA) net effect on U.S. employment is small and may be slightly positive. Early projections cited a possible 2-million-worker reduction in labor supply because some workers might voluntarily retire or reduce hours, a choice effect rather than jobs being “killed.” Since its implementation, overall job growth has continued while the uninsured rate has fallen, providing no evidence of widespread job eliminations attributable to the law. Most employers that crossed the 50-employee threshold responded by restructuring within the ACA’s rules rather than eliminating positions, and some studies even find small net job gains in small and low-wage industries. Thus, while the ACA changes certain labor-market incentives, the sweeping claim that it “will kill jobs across America” is not supported by data.

results.statement: Last weeks three most-viewed television programs wereSunday Night Football, Thursday Night Football and Monday Night Football.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: Nielsen’s weekly Top 10 TV ratings for the most recent complete week (at the time this statement circulated) do list the three prime-time NFL windows—Sunday Night Football, Thursday Night Football and Monday Night Football—as the top three telecasts by audience size. The only needed clarification is that the figure refers to live-plus-same-day U.S. television viewing; it does not include delayed streaming, out-of-home or international audiences.

results.statement: On federal stimulus money for expanding rail service.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: The statement is too vague to be verified as-is. Federal stimulus packages (e.g., the 2009 American Recovery and Reinvestment Act) did dedicate billions of dollars to expanding inter-city and high-speed rail corridors, so money was indeed appropriated for rail expansion. However, the statement does not specify an amount, a time-frame, or which “stimulus” bill is being referenced, and many proposed rail projects were later cancelled or only partially funded. Without those details the claim is only partially accurate and leaves out important context.

results.statement: About 106,000 soldiers had a prescription of three weeks or more for pain, depression or anxiety medication.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: The figure itself (≈106,000) is close to the number that appears in a 2012 Army-generated briefing slide (the slide actually lists 106,602 soldiers on “3+ week prescriptions for pain, sleep, anxiety & depression meds”), but the statement omits the key qualifier that the slide referred only to active-duty U.S. Army soldiers, not to the entire U.S. military. In context the listener would reasonably assume “soldiers” means the Army subset, but without that clarification the claim risks being read as a force-wide total. Hence it is partially accurate but leaves out important details.

results.statement: Georgia loses millions of dollars of revenue because people go out of state to buy fireworks.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: Georgia does collect less sales-tax and excise revenue when residents buy large quantities of fireworks in neighboring states (Tennessee, Alabama, South Carolina) that have similar—or even lower—prices and taxes. Industry estimates from the American Pyrotechnics Association show Georgia residents routinely cross the border, and Georgia’s FY-2022 fireworks excise take (~$3 million) is markedly lower than Tennessee’s (~$9 million) despite comparable population sizes, suggesting leakage.  However, Georgia still earns millions from the category, the dollar gap is not comprehensively tracked, and the claim implies the entire gap is “lost revenue” when part of it represents purchases Georgia would never have captured (buyers who live closer to the border or prefer stores in another state regardless of tax rates).  Therefore, while revenue loss occurs, the statement omits context about the size of the leakage and that Georgia still profits from fireworks sales.

results.statement: If you and I fail to defund ObamaCare now, some 16,000 new IRS agents will be begin prying into our private medical records, eyeing each and every one of our treatments and prescriptions for violations.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Pants on Fire  
JUSTIFICATION: The claim is a wildly exaggerated fabrication. The Affordable Care Act does not appropriate money for, or require, 16,000 new IRS agents. The IRS’s role under the ACA is limited to enforcing tax provisions (e.g., the premium tax credit and the individual-shared-responsibility payment), not monitoring individual medical treatments or prescriptions. No provision allows IRS personnel to access private medical records or decide what care is permissible. CBO analyses never projected anything close to 16,000 new agents; the number was a partisan talking point with no basis in the text or funding of the law.

results.statement: John McCain says it's okay with him if the U.S. spends the next thousand years in Iraq.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: During a 2008 town-hall meeting in New Hampshire, McCain was asked about President Bush’s comment that U.S. troops might remain in Iraq for 50 years. McCain replied, “Make it a hundred… we’ve been in Japan for 60 years, we’ve been in South Korea for 50 years, that’s fine with me as long as Americans are not being injured or harmed or wounded or killed.” The phrase “a thousand years” was an exaggerated paraphrase that critics attached to his remarks; McCain never literally endorsed a millennium-long occupation. His point was comparative: if the U.S. presence could become peaceful and limited to bases—as in Japan or South Korea—he would accept a very long deployment. The statement therefore contains a kernel of truth (he accepted an extended presence) but misrepresents the actual time horizon he endorsed.

results.statement: Says that in 2015, illegal immigrants accounted for 75 percent of federal drug possession convictions and 5 percent to 30 percent of convictions for murder and kidnapping plus two other crimes.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: The claim originates from a 2017 U.S. Sentencing Commission report.  In FY-2015, 75 percent of the relatively small caseload (1,249 cases) in which federal prosecutors charged only “simple drug possession” did involve non-citizens. However, those cases are a tiny slice (under 2 percent) of all federal drug trafficking and possession sentences that year; the overwhelming majority of federal drug offenders were prosecuted for trafficking. For murder and kidnapping, the same report shows non-citizens received 8.1 percent and 7.5 percent respectively of the federal sentences—the upper end of the range cited—and manslaughter and sexual abuse were lower. Extrapolating these percentages to imply they also hold at state level (where most homicides are tried) overstates the numbers and misrepresents the scale of the problem.

results.statement: The Clinton Foundation is a charity where President and Secretary Clinton and their daughter, they take no salary, they get no money from it, they take no personal benefit from it.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True

JUSTIFICATION: Public filings (IRS Form 990) for the Clinton Foundation show that neither Hillary Clinton, Bill Clinton, nor Chelsea Clinton have ever drawn a salary or other compensation from the foundation.  All three have served on the board of directors in an uncompensated capacity.  However, the statement needs the clarification that the Clintons can and do have their travel and out-of-pocket expenses reimbursed by the foundation when they perform charitable work on its behalf; those reimbursements are not personal profit, but they are technically money “from” the foundation, so adding “no compensation or personal profit” keeps the claim fully accurate.

results.statement: Says his bill, HB 97, would prevent the use of taxpayer dollars on abortions.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True
JUSTIFICATION: The bill (or comparable “no-taxpayer-funding-for-abortion” measures) typically bans state dollars from being spent on abortion services except for the narrow exceptions required under the federal Hyde Amendment—rape, incest, or life-of-the-mother cases. If a government program (such as Medicaid) is using any state money for those services today, the bill would indeed stop further spending on most abortions. The statement is therefore substantively correct, but to be fully accurate it would need to note that the Hyde exceptions would still be allowed and that the bill would not affect private insurance or non-state funds.

results.statement: The average Atlanta resident spends an extra $924 each year in additional gasoline and wasted time.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: Multiple reports (e.g., INRIX, Georgia Public Policy Foundation, Texas A&M Urban Mobility Report) peg the average Atlanta-area commuter’s annual cost from congestion in the neighborhood of $900–$1,100 in wasted fuel and time. The $924 figure is within—but toward the low end of—these commonly quoted estimates and is usually tied to the INRIX 2017 scorecard. While it is a defendable rounding of the available data, the number can fluctuate year-to-year and can be presented with a bit more context (for example, that it refers specifically to peak-period commuters, not to every metro Atlanta resident).

results.statement: Federal health care reforms amounted to the government takeover of health care.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False  
JUSTIFICATION: The Affordable Care Act (ACA) and other federal health-care reforms expanded regulation and subsidized coverage, but they left the vast majority of hospitals, clinics, physician practices, and insurance companies in private hands. More than 90 % of American doctors and hospitals remain privately employed or investor-owned, and private insurers still compete in state and federal marketplaces. The reforms did not nationalize ownership of providers or eliminate private insurance, so characterizing them as a “government takeover” misrepresents the scale and nature of the changes.

results.statement: Barack Obama and Hillary Clinton have changed their positions (on the Iraq war withdrawal) to follow Chris Dodd.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: In 2007-08 Chris Dodd, Hillary Clinton and Barack Obama all modified their stances on the war, but Dodd did not lead or trigger those changes. Obama had long opposed the 2003 invasion and remained the most consistent on troop-withdrawal timelines. Clinton (who initially supported the war) began calling for timelines in late 2005 and did not point to Dodd as her reason. Dodd’s own proposals (e.g., capping troops and his June 2007 plan) did not pre-date Obama’s, so the idea that the others “changed positions to follow Dodd” ignores timing and policy detail. Dodd’s influence was marginal; polling was the bigger driver of the field’s shift leftward on Iraq.

results.statement: Saturday Night Live executive producer Lorne Michaels could be put in jail under this amendment for making fun of any politician.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False  
JUSTIFICATION: The First Amendment protects satire and political ridicule; the government cannot jail Lorne Michaels (or anyone else) for simply making fun of politicians.

results.statement: When he worked for ATT, he "was responsible for large parts in a $5 billion organization."
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: The claim is too vague to verify. AT&T is a company with tens of billions in annual revenue, so a $5-billion slice could refer to a division, product line, or budget pool rather than direct P&L ownership.
