Without knowing the person’s title, span of control, head-count, budget authority, or time period, we can’t confirm he personally “was responsible for large parts.” The dollar figure may be technically attached to the unit he worked in, but the wording inflates his personal accountability.

results.statement: 6,400 Ohioans ... lost manufacturing jobs in the month of September.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics monthly state employment report shows that seasonally-adjusted Ohio manufacturing employment fell from 691,200 in August to 684,800 in September 2023—a drop of 6,400 jobs. The figure is accurate for that month, but the statement omits the usual caveat that monthly preliminary state data can be revised and that September’s loss followed four consecutive months of manufacturing gains, so the single-month change does not reflect the longer-term trend.

results.statement: Mitch McConnell voted with Harry Reid to infringe on our gun rights.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: While both Mitch McConnell and Harry Reid have on occasion supported the same gun-related procedural votes—most notably the 2013 vote to begin debate on the Manchin-Toomey background-check amendment—their ultimate positions were opposite. Reid voted to end debate and pass the bill; McConnell ultimately voted against the measure and against cloture. The claim implies a joint vote to restrict rights, which is misleading because McConnell opposed the underlying proposal and voted in the direction most gun-rights supporters preferred.

results.statement: Says Ron Johnson gave himself a $10 million sweetheart corporate payout
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False

JUSTIFICATION: Ron Johnson left his role as CEO of PACUR (a plastics company founded by his brother-in-law) in 1997 when he entered the State Senate. In 2010, shortly before running for U.S. Senate, the privately held firm paid him a one-time deferred-compensation package of between $9 million and $10 million. The payout was approved by the other shareholders (all family) after Johnson spent 13 years in the state legislature while receiving a below-market salary from the firm. While critics characterize it as a “sweetheart” payout, the structure was legal and approved by all owners, so labeling it money Johnson “gave himself” overstates his personal control of the transaction.

results.statement: Of the roughly 15 percent of Americans who dont have health insurance, half of them made more than $50,000 a year.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: Roughly 8–9 % of the U.S. population under 65 was uninsured in recent Census/AHIP data, not 15 %. Among the uninsured, about 40 % live in households with incomes above 400 % of the federal poverty level—roughly $60 k for a couple or $90 k+ for a family of four. Simply citing “$50 k” therefore overstates the share of higher-income uninsured and uses an outdated national uninsured rate.

results.statement: The United States can immediately tap a domestic energy resource of more than 1.5 trillion barrels of oil, six times more than Saudi Arabia.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: The claim invokes the roughly 1.5-trillion-barrel figure that appears in U.S. Geological Survey and RAND studies for the total oil (and bitumen) locked in U.S. oil-shale deposits. That number is for kerogen-bearing rock, not liquid petroleum. Producing those barrels would require mining or in-situ retorting plants that do not yet exist at commercial scale; none of it is "immediately" available. Current proved U.S. liquid-oil reserves are about 70 billion barrels, far below Saudi Arabia’s roughly 270 billion barrels of proved conventional reserves. So the statement seizes an in-place resource number, ignores technical and economic recoverability, and misrepresents it as ready-to-use barrels—making it mostly wrong.

results.statement: Says House Democrats voted to use your tax dollars for abortions by voting against bill defunding Planned Parenthood.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: The claim rests on the assumption that any money that goes to Planned Parenthood necessarily “funds abortion.” In reality, under the Hyde Amendment (and parallel state laws) federal Medicaid dollars and other federal health-program funds may not be used for elective abortions. Planned Parenthood’s federal reimbursements are for contraception, cancer screening, STI tests, and other non-abortion care. When House Democrats vote against “defunding” measures they are voting to keep those streams of federal payment for the non-abortion services; they are not voting to spend tax money on abortions. Thus the statement takes a kernel of truth—Democrats did vote against the defunding bill—and misrepresents what that vote does.

results.statement: We need a Senator who shows up to work. Sherrod Brown missed over 350 official votes.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: The number itself—"missed over 350 official votes"—is roughly correct (through late 2023 Brown has sat out about 370 recorded votes during his 17-year Senate tenure). But the claim’s framing implies chronic absenteeism. Spread across 4,800+ roll-call votes, Brown’s lifetime absence rate is ≈7.5 %, only marginally higher than the chamber average. Most of the missed votes are procedural or when he was campaigning in-state, and during the same period his vote participation in 2021-22 was 97 %. Presenting raw missed votes without context of total opportunities or comparison to peers gives a misleading impression of dereliction.

results.statement: It is already in the law that there is a requirement to screen (refugees) for religion.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: U.S. immigration law does require that the federal government consider whether an asylum-seeker or refugee has been persecuted “on account of … religion,” but that is a test of whether the person faces religious persecution—not a blanket “screening for religion” that would categorically bar or admit people simply because of which faith they profess. The statute does not mandate religious “screening” in the sense of filtering applicants by their personal belief system; it only obliges the government to assess whether the applicant has a well-founded fear of persecution on protected grounds, one of which is religion. The wording of the claim therefore leaves out the crucial context that the requirement is about adjudicating persecution claims, not about vetting people’s religion per se.

results.statement: At the mere request from Putin, President Obama withdrew the plans for a missile defense program based in Poland and the Czech Republic.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: President Obama replaced the Bush-era plan for fixed ground-based interceptors in Poland and a radar in the Czech Republic with a different, more mobile missile-defense architecture (the European Phased Adaptive Approach, EPAA). The change was publicly justified on internal U.S. strategic reviews that questioned the old scheme’s feasibility against the evolving Iranian missile threat, not on a Russian “request.” Moscow welcomed the move, but U.S. and allied statements of the time, later de-classified memos, and contemporary news coverage all show the decision was made in Washington for U.S./NATO reasons, not as a concession demanded by Putin.

results.statement: I did very well with young people in Ohio, Massachusetts, California.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: There is no publicly available, consistent evidence that the speaker “did very well” with young voters (typically defined as 18-29) in Ohio, Massachusetts, or California. Exit-poll and precinct-level analyses from recent general and primary elections show the speaker generally losing the youth vote in these states—often by large margins. While the claim contains an element of subjective boasting, the objective electoral data do not support the assertion of strong youth support in any of the three named states.

results.statement: Health care reform "would bring down the deficit by as much as $1 trillion over the next two decades."
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: The 2010 Affordable Care Act (ACA) was projected by the non-partisan Congressional Budget Office (CBO) in July 2012 to reduce cumulative federal deficits over the 2013-2022 decade by roughly $109 billion, and over the 2013-2032 period by about $1.25 trillion. Those estimates, however, assume all ACA spending cuts and revenue-raisers (such as Medicare payment reductions, new taxes on high-income households, and the Cadillac plan tax) remain in place. Congress has since repealed or softened several of these provisions—notably the Cadillac tax—so the full theoretical savings are no longer on track. Thus the statement cites a CBO figure that was valid when made, but omits the subsequent legislative changes that make the upper-end $1-trillion saving unlikely.

results.statement: McCain "has opposed stem cell research."
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False
JUSTIFICATION: During his 2008 presidential campaign John McCain repeatedly said he favored federal funding for embryonic stem-cell research, provided it used embryos originally created for fertility treatments that would otherwise be discarded. He voted twice (in 2005 and 2007) for stem-cell research bills that President Bush ultimately vetoed. McCain did express moral qualms about embryo destruction, but his legislative record and public statements show support—not opposition—to the research. Saying he “opposed” it ignores those votes and campaign pledges.

results.statement: Austin has more lobbyists working for it than any other municipality in Texas.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True  
JUSTIFICATION: Austin is the largest Texas city that still contracts with private lobbying firms to represent its interests at the state legislature, and state-disclosure reports show that it spends more on contract lobbyists than any other municipality in Texas. However, the city of Houston maintains its own in-house governmental-relations office whose employees register as lobbyists; because those staff salaries are not reflected in expenditure-based “lobbying” totals, Houston’s total lobbying workforce is actually larger. Austin therefore has the highest contract-lobbyist spending, but not necessarily the largest number of registered lobbyists once in-house staff lobbyists are counted.

results.statement: The EPA wants to hire 230,000 new government regulators that will cost the taxpayer $21 billion.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: False  
JUSTIFICATION: The claim is based on a distorted reading of the Environmental Protection Agency’s draft “Workforce Strategy for the Inflation Reduction Act.” The document lists ~230,000 total new positions (“job-years”) that could be supported by the IRA’s environmental-programs budget over the next decade—i.e., a cumulative tally of temporary, state, local, tribal, and private-sector jobs, not 230,000 new permanent federal regulators. EPA’s own FY-2024 budget request shows a planned federal payroll increase of only a few thousand FTEs, nowhere near six figures, and no line item obligates $21 billion just for their salaries.

results.statement: If the Trans-Pacific Partnership is fast-tracked through the Senate, it wont receive a committee assignment and the consideration will include no debate.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True
JUSTIFICATION: The statement misrepresents how fast-track (Trade Promotion Authority) works. Under fast-track rules the Senate does not skip the committee stage; the bill is automatically referred to the Finance Committee, which must report it within a set number of days. What fast-track does is bar amendments and impose strict time limits on floor debate—up to 20 hours of debate equally divided—before a final up-or-down vote. Thus the agreement will receive a committee assignment and there will be floor debate, although the debate is limited and no amendments are allowed.

results.statement: On whether the government should bail out insurance giant AIG.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Pants on Fire

JUSTIFICATION: The statement is not actually a falsifiable claim—it's merely a topic or question, not an assertion of fact. Therefore it cannot be assigned any truth value; treating it as a factual claim is itself nonsensical.

results.statement: The Cleveland police has issued a stand down order to officers.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly False  
JUSTIFICATION: No credible contemporary source—no statement from the City of Cleveland, the Cleveland Division of Police, or any major Ohio news outlet—indicates that a “stand-down order” (i.e., an instruction for officers to avoid proactive enforcement or not respond to calls) has been issued. The claim has circulated only on fringe social-media accounts and a few blogs, none of which have produced a document, directive, or on-record official to substantiate it. Therefore, while the assertion is in wide circulation, it is unsupported and leaves a false impression.

results.statement: The Keystone pipeline creates 35 permanent jobs after construction, according to a government report.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Mostly True

JUSTIFICATION:  
A 2014 Final Environmental Impact Statement prepared for the U.S. Department of State (the lead federal reviewer) estimated that once the proposed Keystone XL pipeline was built and operating, it would require about 35 permanent employees—roughly 15 for pipeline operation and 20 for the six pump-station facilities. These are full-time, year-round jobs, not seasonal or short-term positions. That figure has been cited in many subsequent government and news summaries and matches the number given in the statement. While the project also supports thousands of temporary construction (and some indirect) jobs, the 35 permanent number is specific to long-term operational staff.

Because the statement omits context about temporary vs. permanent roles and does not mention the source of the figure, a small amount of clarification is needed, but the core claim is supported by a federal report.

results.statement: We already pay the highest electricity prices in the country here in New England.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: True  
JUSTIFICATION: U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) state-level data consistently show Connecticut, Massachusetts, Rhode Island, New Hampshire, Vermont and Maine at or near the top of the 48 mainland states for average residential and commercial retail electricity prices (¢/kWh). For 2023, every New England state except Maine ranked in the top-10 most expensive; Connecticut and Massachusetts were Nos. 1 and 2. Because the region pays the highest (or tied-for-highest) prices in the nation year after year, the blanket statement “We already pay the highest electricity prices in the country here in New England” is accurate.

results.statement: We're going to have more troops (in Afghanistan) . . . than the Russians had.
results.raw_response:
VERDICT: Half True  
JUSTIFICATION: At the 2010–2011 peak the United States had roughly 100,000 troops in Afghanistan.  Soviet troop strength during their 1979-1989 war peaked at about 115,000-120,000.  Because the U.S. contingent never exceeded that Soviet peak, the literal claim is inaccurate.  However, when the statement was made (late 2009) the announced surge would bring U.S. force levels to roughly the same order of magnitude as the historic Soviet maximum, so the remark captures the scale-up but over-states the comparative size.
